3. **Zero-retention providers** — Many providers are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others. Filter to them the same way as training policy.

# The Maloha Coast Navigator — Test Edition

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca4c3e7e0cefc
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Self-BCC to sayf's inbox

**The Maloha Coast Navigator**  
*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

---

## Client Spotlight

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

---

## What We're Reading

- [[Claude Code 10x Skill]] — The skill pattern that changed everything
- [[Second Brain Architecture]] — How we structure knowledge for SMBs

---

## Upcoming

Next week: Pricing models that work for SMB AI consulting.

*— Sayf, Maloha Coast*

# The 3-Folder System: Atlas → Projects → End Products (Head, Hands, Heart)

**Source:** YouTube — "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture"
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rl7rvHbDqGk
**Format:** Video transcript
**Ingested:** 2026-06-05

## Summary

The speaker discovered that disorganized AI outputs were a reflection of disorganized thinking. After months of building AI agents and burning API tokens, he stopped and realized the real problem wasn't the tools — it was his cognitive architecture. His core insight: **your file structure is a map of your thinking**. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar.

The solution is embarrassingly simple: a three-folder system that maps to Head, Hands, and Heart.

## The Three-Folder Framework

### 1. Atlas (Head / Thinking)
Raw materials, ideas, strategy, capture. Where you put ideas when you get them — the thinking layer. Everything that isn't yet a project.

### 2. Projects (Hands / Building)
Active work. Content being created, things being built. This is the doing layer.

### 3. End Products (Heart / Serving)
Completed work. Finished deliverables that serve other people. The output layer.

## Key Insights

- **File structure = cognitive architecture.** Your external files reveal your internal thinking patterns.
- **Organization is speed.** [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor): "Organization is heaven's first law." Clear structure makes AI faster and cheaper (fewer tokens).
- **The sophistication trap.** Smart people overcomplicate things. The answer is usually simpler than you think.
- **AI reveals you to yourself.** AI doesn't just do thinking FOR you — it shows you HOW you think by externalizing your patterns.
- **Agents mirror human growth.** An AI agent needs identity, context, and skills — the same three things a human needs to change their life.
- **Stop building, start organizing.** Before chasing the next tool or agent, fix the foundation.

## Claude Mirrors the Same Structure

| Mode | Purpose | Maps To |
|------|---------|---------|
| Chat | Thinking, exploring ideas | Atlas (Head) |
| Co-work | Building artifacts, plans | Projects (Hands) |
| Claude Code | Finished products, shipped work | End Products (Heart) |

## The Three Communities Pattern

The speaker's Skool communities also follow this structure:
- **Free community** (Lifestyle Founders Group) → Thinking, sharing ideas (Atlas)
- **Paid community** (Claude for Coaches) → Active building, workshops (Projects)
- **VIP community** → End products, completed work (Heart)

## Notable Quotes

- "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar."
- "Organization is heaven's first law." — [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor)
- "The bottleneck isn't the tool, it's the clarity of your thinking. Your cognitive architecture is now your competitive advantage."
- "Fix the inside, the outside follows. As above, so below."
- "I need a second brain sounds nice, but actually my first brain needs to get organized first."

## People/Resources Mentioned

- [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor) — "Organization is heaven's first law"
- [Jake Van Cleef](people/jake-van-cleef) — academic paper on stopping agent building, fixing file structure first
- [Nick Milo](people/nick-milo) — Obsidian/second brain advocate, three-folder patterns
- Head, Hands & Heart Notes: https://imported-abacus-8dd.notion.site/
- Free Skool Community: https://skool.com/lfg
- Claude For Coaches: https://www.skool.com/claudeforcoaches/about
- Lifestyle Founder Mastermind: https://www.skool.com/quantum100/about

[Source: YouTube video, ingested 2026-06-05]

pages.title: Virtual Machines Deep Dive
pages.body:
# How Virtual Machines Work - A Deep Dive (#148: Part 1 - DevOps Mastermind)

**From:** [[Neo Kim]] (via systemdesignone@substack.com, forwarded by Sayf Codes)
**Date:** Mon, 25 May 2026
**Source:** email forward

---

**DevOps Mastermind** — A newsletter series to help engineers master DevOps fundamentals instead of just memorizing commands. Architecture breakdowns of core DevOps systems, deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and practical insights into scalability, automation, and reliability.

---

## Emulation vs Virtualization

Emulation is when software pretends to be completely different hardware (e.g., retro gaming emulators). Each instruction from the old system is translated for the modern chip. It works, but it's slow.

Virtualization creates a version of the same hardware the machine already has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor. Result: near-native speed.

- Emulation = hiring a translator for every sentence
- Virtualization = both people already speak the same language

This speed is what made virtualization practical at scale — you cannot run thousands of emulated systems on a single server. With virtualization, you can.

## What a Virtual Machine Actually Is

A VM is a complete computer abstraction built on top of a physical machine. It's presented with CPU, memory, storage, and network — just like a physical system. The OS inside has no awareness it's sharing the machine.

**The hypervisor** is the layer that makes the illusion possible. Every request for CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network goes through it. Three key capabilities:

1. **Isolation** — each VM runs independently; a failure in one doesn't affect others
2. **Portability** — VMs can move between physical systems with minimal changes
3. **Efficient resource use** — multiple workloads share the same hardware

## Hypervisor: Brain Behind the Illusion

### Type 1 vs Type 2

| Type | Description | Examples |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Type 1 (bare-metal)** | Runs directly on physical hardware, no host OS in between | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM |
| **Type 2 (hosted)** | Runs on top of an existing OS like a regular app | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |

### Full Virtualization vs Paravirtualization

- **Full virtualization**: Guest OS runs unmodified; hypervisor handles all abstraction work
- **Paravirtualization**: Guest OS is aware it's virtualized and cooperates with the hypervisor to reduce overhead
- Trade-off: compatibility (full) vs performance (para)

### Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

Intel VT-x and AMD-V let the CPU itself differentiate between VMs and the hypervisor. Sensitive operations pass control to the hypervisor automatically. This made virtualization practical at scale.

### Nested Virtualization

Running a VM inside another VM. Useful for testing and local Kubernetes setups but unsuitable for production due to overhead.

## CPU Virtualization

### The Privilege Model

In a normal system, the OS sits at the highest privilege level. In a virtual environment, the hypervisor needs to sit above the OS. The guest OS gets moved down a level — but never knows it was demoted.

When the guest tries a privileged operation, the processor hands control to the hypervisor.

### vCPU and Scheduling

When you create a VM, you assign vCPUs (virtual CPUs). The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical cores via **time-slicing** — the same way an OS runs multiple apps.

**CPU overcommit**: Assigning more vCPUs than physical cores (e.g., 16 cores running 32 vCPUs). Works if all VMs aren't busy simultaneously. When demand rises, contention reduces performance.

### VM-exit and VM-entry

- **VM-exit**: VM tries a privileged instruction → processor stops it → control transfers to hypervisor
- **VM-entry**: Hypervisor handles the request → hands control back to the VM exactly where it left off
- Handoff takes microseconds. But frequent exits hurt performance.

### NUMA Awareness (Non-Uniform Memory Access)

Modern servers have multiple processors, each with local memory. Accessing memory on a different processor is slower. The hypervisor tries to keep a VM's vCPUs and memory on the same NUMA node.

For most small deployments this doesn't matter. For high-performance databases on large servers, it matters a lot.

---

|*This was a free preview of a subscriber-only post from [[Neo Kim]]'s "The System Design Newsletter". Guest author: [[Ayaan]] (self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure).*

## Key Takeaways

- Virtualization enabled cloud computing by splitting one powerful machine into many isolated ones
- The hypervisor is the core abstraction layer — handles CPU, memory, storage, and networking
- Hardware support (VT-x/AMD-V) made virtualization practical at scale
- Containers are a different approach (OS-level virtualization, shared kernel) — covered in Part 1's comparison

## People Mentioned

- **[[Neo Kim]]** — Author of The System Design Newsletter (systemdesignone.substack.com). Wrote this deep dive on virtualization architecture as part of the DevOps Mastermind series.
- **[[Ayaan]]** — Self-taught DevOps engineer, guest author. Focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure. Builds in public on Twitter/X and GitHub.

pages.title: Vps Hardening
pages.body:
# VPS Hardening

## Overview
Security hardening of the Maloha Coast production VPS (Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124-generic). All changes applied and verified post-reboot.

## Changes Applied

### SSH Hardening
| Setting | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| PermitRootLogin | yes | prohibit-password |
| X11Forwarding | yes | no |
| MaxAuthTries | 6 (default) | 3 |
| ClientAliveInterval | none | 300s (5 min) |
| ClientAliveCountMax | 3 (default) | 2 |
| AllowUsers | any local user | ubuntu only |
| DSA host keys | present | removed |
| PasswordAuthentication | yes | no |

### Firewall (UFW)
- **Default**: deny all incoming, allow all outgoing
- **Open ports**: 22/tcp (SSH) only
- Status: active

### fail2ban
- **Jail**: sshd (aggressive mode)
- **Max retries**: 3
- **Ban time**: 1 hour
- **Post-reboot**: banned 4 IPs within 2 minutes of coming online
- See [[2026-06-03#VPS Hardening]] for timeline

### System Updates
- 78 packages upgraded
- Kernel updated to 6.8.0-124 (was pending reboot, now active)

## Verification
- [x] SSH config validated via `sshd -T`
- [x] UFW active with correct rules
- [x] fail2ban running with active bans
- [x] Hermes gateway auto-starts on boot
- [x] Ubuntu NOPASSWD sudo preserved (Hermes requirement)

## Related
- [[Google Workspace Setup]]
- [[2026-06-03]]
- [[Gateway Infrastructure]] — gateway health monitoring and watchdog

pages.title: The 15-Minute Gap Method
pages.body:
Zaim here. Wanted to capture this before I forget.

Between KSI sessions I have these 15-minute gaps. Started using them to jot down one thing I noticed about each student — what they're stuck on, what lit them up. After a month of this, patterns emerged that I'd never have spotted otherwise.

Three buckets emerged:
1. Career pivoters — stuck on 'what if I fail' not 'what do I want'
2. Fresh grads — over-indexing on salary, under-indexing on growth trajectory  
3. Mid-career — all variations of 'is this it?'

The 15-minute rule: never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds.

— Zaim

pages.title: Zaim 3month Career Pivot Framework
pages.body:
## The 3-Month Career Pivot Framework

Zaim shared this during a coaching session with a KSI senior analyst looking to move into product.

### Phase 1: Audit (Month 1)
- [[Skills Inventory]] — map current skills to target role requirements
- Identify the 20% skill gap that blocks 80% of interviews
- Don't learn everything — learn the gatekeepers

### Phase 2: Signal (Month 2)
- [[Side Project Strategy]] — build one public artifact, not ten private ones
- Write 2 LinkedIn posts/week documenting the pivot journey
- Engage with 3 target-company hiring managers

### Phase 3: Convert (Month 3)
- [[Salary Negotiation Playbook]] — comp bands for product roles in KL
- Reference calls with 2 people who made the same pivot
- First offer within 90 days

### Key Insight
> 'The career pivot gap isn't a skills problem — it's a positioning problem. You're not underqualified, you're framed wrong.' — Zaim

**Client:** KSI Senior Analyst → Product Manager
**Engagement:** Weekly coaching, 12 sessions
**Status:** Phase 2, on track

͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏

͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­


It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered
(and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by...the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.
The annual goal trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on.

"Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing
happens.

By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything
that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for
next year.

I went through this myself but with weight, not career. In January 2025, I
set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Looked fine
on paper.

Then I came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week
Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years. They
operate in the last few weeks before a deadline, however long that deadline
is. So the fix is to shrink the deadline.

Btw, this book is available on Shopee!

I rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March.

I hit it!!

The best part was that I never had to set that goal again. The habits I
built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year.
Why this works for your career too

The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot.

"Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday.

"By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target
department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to
leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday.

One of these is a wish. The other is a plan.

I've worked with clients on exactly this. The ones who set a 90-day goal
like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached,
mostly hit it or get close. The ones with "find a better job this year"
mostly don't, because there's no Monday task attached to it.
How to set yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one.

Follow this format and write down:

   - *My 90-day career goal is...*
   - *This matters to me because...*
   - *To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have...*
   (make this checkable. "3 offers" is checkable. "More clarity" is not.)
   - *To make sure I complete it, I'm going to...* (weekly actions, not the
   outcome)

Your homework this weekend

Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey
area.

Then build it into your week, not your year.
------------------------------

P.S. I'm running a free workshop exactly on setting this 90-day goal for Q3
2026. If you're interested to join, it's happening early next month in
June. For more info or to sign up, head over to
https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6
<https://preview.kit-mail3.com/click/dpheh0hzhmh4/aHR0cHM6Ly90Lmx5L2dldGNsZWFyb25jYXJlZXI2>

Unsubscribe <https://preview.kit-mail3.com/unsubscribe> | Update your
profile <https://preview.kit-mail3.com/preferences> | I Write on LinkedIn
Every Day - Follow Me For Career, AI and Productivity Insights
<https://preview.kit-mail3.com/click/dpheh0hzhmh4/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGlua2VkaW4uY29tL2luL3phaW1tb2h6YW5pLw==>

pages.title: Zaim Pipeline Fix Test 20260615
pages.body:
# End-to-End Pipeline Test (Post-Fix Verification)

**Source:** Pipeline test — verifying Bug 1 (venv Python) and Bug 2 (YAML tags) fixes.

## Context

This page tests the full email-to-gbrain pipeline after applying two critical fixes discovered on June 15, 2026:

1. **Bug 1 — Cron Python PATH**: Pipeline now uses Hermes venv Python at `/home/ubuntu/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv/bin/python` instead of bare `python`/`python3`.

2. **Bug 2 — Tags Lost on Export**: Workspace files now embed `tags:` in YAML frontmatter so the `zaim` tag survives `gbrain export` and reaches the enrichment pipeline.

## Verification Steps

- [ ] Workspace file saved with YAML frontmatter
- [ ] `gbrain put --tags zaim --body-stdin` succeeds
- [ ] `gbrain export` includes `tags:` in YAML frontmatter
- [ ] Enrichment pipeline picks up the `zaim` tag
- [ ] Dashboard shows this page under the Zaim filter

**Test timestamp:** $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca0a8dc4888c3
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded to Zaim's workspace via self-BCC

Zaim here. Adding a quick profile on a new client.

Name: Aisyah Rahim
Role: Senior Brand Manager at Unilever Malaysia
Context: Referred by Liyana from the KSI workshop last month. First session next Tuesday.

Key points from our intro call:
- 8 years in FMCG brand management, feeling stagnant
- Wants to pivot into tech but unsure where to start
- Main blocker: 'I don't have a technical background'
- Has actually done more data-driven work than she realises — ran consumer analytics for 3 product launches
- Salary anchor: RM 18k/month, worried about resetting to junior level

My read: Classic mid-career pivot case. The real problem isn't skills — it's confidence and narrative. She needs help reframing 'brand manager' as 'growth strategist who uses data.' Tech companies would kill for someone who can bridge consumer insights and product.

Plan for session 1: Audit her last 2 years of work through a tech lens. Find 3-5 specific examples where she already did 'product thinking' without calling it that.

— Zaim

pages.title: Gateway Infrastructure
pages.body:
# Gateway Infrastructure

## Hardware
2GB VPS (Tencent Cloud, Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124). 2GB swap. ~50GB storage.

## Hermes Gateway

### Service
Systemd user service at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service`. Auto-starts on boot. Linger enabled so it survives logout.

### Exit Code 75 (EX_TEMPFAIL)
The gateway uses exit code 75 as a "please restart me" signal to systemd. Triggered by `/restart`, `/update`, or SIGUSR1. The systemd config has `RestartForceExitStatus=75` so this always triggers a restart. See [[gateway-watchdog]] for crash-loop detection.

### Memory Profile
- Baseline: ~97MB RSS at startup
- After 14h moderate use: ~600MB (growth ~36MB/h)
- Peak observed: 1.0G
- Gradual leak under active use — tracked by watchdog growth-rate alert

### Manual Recovery
When stuck in a crash loop: `sudo -u ubuntu /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/hermes gateway start`

## Gateway Health Watchdog

Created Jun 4, 2026. A Python script at `~/.hermes/scripts/gateway-watchdog.py` that runs every 10 minutes via cron (`no_agent=True`, zero LLM tokens). Silent when healthy; alerts Discord #Home when thresholds are breached.

### Thresholds
- Gateway RSS >= 1.4GB (warning) / >= 1.6GB (critical)
- System free memory <= 300MB (warning) / <= 150MB (critical)
- Swap usage > 200MB
- >= 1 exit code 75 crash in last hour
- >= 1 SIGTERM kill in last hour
- Gateway uptime < 30 min
- Memory growth rate > 50MB/hour

### Related
- [[VPS Hardening]] — SSH, firewall, fail2ban baseline
- [[2026-06-04]] — watchdog setup and gateway crash investigation

pages.title: Hermes Profile Backup
pages.body:
## Hermes Profile Backup

The Hermes profile backup pushes all agent profiles to a private GitHub repo with secrets stripped.

### Repository
- **Repo:** `github.com/Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup` (private)
- **Contents:** 1,186 files across 2 profiles (default + MC)
- **Includes:** SOUL.md, skills, cron, memories, config.yaml (stripped), setup.sh, .gitignore

### What gets backed up
- SOUL.md (agent identity)
- skills/ (all skill files)
- cron/ (scheduled jobs)
- memories/ (USER.md + MEMORY.md)
- config.yaml with API keys replaced by <YOUR_KEY_HERE>

### What gets EXCLUDED
- API keys, tokens, secrets (replaced with placeholders)
- Session logs and databases
- OAuth tokens (google_token.json)
- Runtime state files

### Restore
```bash
git clone git@github.com:Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup.git
cd hermes-profile-backup
./setup.sh
```
Then edit ~/.hermes/config.yaml and ~/.hermes/.env with credentials.

### Known gap
- .env.template is missing from the current backup (README references it but file was not generated). Needs to be added on next backup run.

[[HumanLayer]] has launched publicly. It's an Agentic IDE and collaboration platform for engineering teams to design complex systems with AI agents before generating code.

### Key Details

- **Product:** [[HumanLayer]] — Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and building blocks for the software factory
- **Pricing:** Starter plan free for teams of fewer than three
- **URL:** [humanlayer.com](https://humanlayer.com)
- **Launch post (blurb):** [humanlayer.com/launch/blurb.md](https://humanlayer.com/launch/blurb.md)
- **X post:** [@dexhorthy launch thread](https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/2067286892786454855)
- **LinkedIn:** [[Dex Horthy]] launch post

### Core Thesis

> The year of no more slop.

[[HumanLayer]]'s core idea: **don't outsource the thinking.** Great teams have run design reviews for decades because there's enormous leverage in collaborating on system design *before* writing the code. [[HumanLayer]] brings that missing piece of the software factory into one place.

### What It Does

- Lets engineers move 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC (not just coding)
- Maintains rigorous standards for system architecture and program design
- Engineers design complex systems with AI agents **before** generating a single line of code
- **BYO subscription** — plug in Claude, Codex, and other AI subscriptions/API keys; no separate per-token bill

### Background

[[Dex Horthy]] and [[Kyle]] (the founders) previously built the open-source Research, Plan, Implement (RPI) framework, now running inside Fortune 500s like Block and Uber. [[HumanLayer]] is built on top of all that work.

### People Mentioned

pages.title: Msg 19ee03492356403f
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** OpenRouter Team (newsletter)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20
**Provenance:** Email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

[Provider policy table](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/privacy-and-logging)

## What you control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** Account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override account default on any single call via [data policy filtering](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing#requiring-providers-to-comply-with-data-policies).
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers — including Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, and Together — are zero retention. Filter to them the same way as training policy.

## If you need stricter guarantees

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. Prompts and completions processed inside the EU via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`. [Contact enterprise team](https://openrouter.ai/enterprise/form) to enable it.

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models.

pages.title: How provider data policies work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-19
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f

## Summary

[[OpenRouter]] provides structured data about each provider's retention and training policies, publishes a full table in their docs, and gives users three layers of control over which providers can process their prompts.

## Key Content

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint and publishes the full table in the docs.

### What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** An account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. If you opt out, [[OpenRouter]] will not route to training providers.

2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override your account default on any single call via data policy filtering in the provider routing docs.

3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

### Enterprise Options

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`.

## Links

- Provider policy table: https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/privacy-and-logging
- Privacy settings: https://openrouter.ai/settings/privacy
- Data policy filtering docs: https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing
- Enterprise: https://openrouter.ai/enterprise/form

pages.title: Openrouter Three Features 2026 06 18
pages.body:
# Three features worth trying next on OpenRouter

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T03:30:00Z

OpenRouter onboarding email covering three features that are single-parameter changes with production value.

## Key Points

1. **Streaming** (`stream=True`): Tokens arrive as generated. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

2. **Structured outputs** (`response_format` with JSON schema): OpenRouter enforces JSON schema at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops for malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the format; router respects it with `openrouter/auto`.

3. **Provider preferences** (`provider` param in `extra_body`): Pin specific providers, exclude others, set latency floors and throughput minimums. Sort by `"price"` to always pick the cheapest, or use an allowlist to lock routing to trusted providers.

## Combined Example

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openrouter/auto",
    stream=True,
    response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
    extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}],
)
```

## Useful Pages

- [Models & pricing](https://openrouter.ai/models)
- [Rankings (real usage volume)](https://openrouter.ai/rankings)
- [Usage & costs](https://openrouter.ai/activity)

pages.title: Three features worth trying next on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# Three features worth trying next on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-18
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0

## Summary

Three [[OpenRouter]] features that are each a single parameter change, don't require a new integration or endpoint, and each earns its place in production code.

## Key Features

### 1. Streaming

Set `stream=True` and tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth, one parameter. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time — the difference between a chat UI that feels alive and one that feels broken. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

### 2. Structured Outputs

Pass a JSON schema via `response_format` and [[OpenRouter]] enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops when the model returns malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the parameter.

### 3. Provider Preferences

Use the `provider` parameter to control which providers handle a request: set `ignore=`` to skip specific providers, set `order` to prioritize by latency or cost, or set `allow_fallbacks` to keep the request reliable even when preferred providers are down.

pages.title: Graphify Solves Claude Code Problem
pages.body:
# Graphify Solves Claude Code's Problem

**Source:** YouTube video by Jack, AI automation channel
**URL:** https://youtu.be/Owv503rTqYY
**Ingested:** 2026-06-09
**Type:** tutorial + product pitch

## Executive Summary

Graphify is a tool that creates a knowledge graph of any codebase — mapping files, modules, "god nodes" (load-bearing files), and their interdependencies. This gives Claude (or any AI agent) a persistent, queryable map so it doesn't re-read the entire repo every conversation. The core insight: "rereading is the tax we're saving" — the graph lets the AI answer from summaries instead of re-parsing flat code. When connected to an agentic operating system (Hermes + Claude Code + a visual dashboard), it becomes a shared brain that compounds across sessions and devices.

## The Core Idea

Graphify treats every codebase as a foreign country. Without it, you're translating a language you don't speak. With it, you have a map — you understand relationships, dependencies, and what affects what before editing anything.

### What the Map Does
- **Instant orientation** — understand any repo immediately without reading through it
- **Grounded answers** — queries pull from the graph, not guesses from skimming
- **Blast radius awareness** — see every dependency before editing a file
- **Token savings** — avoid re-reading the repo each conversation; answer from summaries
- **Always fresh** — the graph is regenerated, not stale

### How It Works
1. Reads code (does not summarize)
2. Clusters things into modules
3. Ranks "god nodes" — load-bearing files
4. Labels facts vs. guesses (extracted vs. inferred)

## Technical Architecture

- **Input:** Any codebase (Claude Code, Hermes, GitHub repos)
- **Output:** Knowledge graph with interactive visualization (community clusters)
- **Integration:** Works as a Claude Code skill; connects to Hermes Agent
- **Dashboard:** Visual interface showing files, links, clusters, and cost savings per conversation

## The Agentic Operating System Layer

The video's key argument is that Graphify alone is useful, but Graphify + an agentic OS is the 10x unlock. The OS provides:

- **One shared brain:** Graphify, Claude Code, Hermes, and the dashboard all read from one registry
- **Cross-device continuity:** Work on laptop with Claude Code, then query from mobile via Hermes — same graph
- **Any repo ingestion:** Paste a GitHub URL → "graphify it" → $0 cost → queryable immediately
- **Overnight reviews:** The OS runs dream cycles, searches for improvements, processes chat history
- **Hermes Telegram integration:** Hermes can reach out proactively with questions

## Key Quotes

- "Rereading is the tax that we're saving here" (3:04)
- "Without the map, it skims a whole repo every single conversation" (3:10)
- "With the map, we can actually answer from summaries — every session compounds" (3:13)
- "Graphify makes the map and the OS makes it always on, shared, and conversational" (7:02)
- "If you're not using an agentic operating system, you're leaving so much value on the table" (10:04)

## People Mentioned

- **Jack** — Video creator, AI automation YouTuber. Previously built and sold a tech startup "with a gazillion customers." Now building AI businesses. Runs a community with Claude Code + Hermes operating system templates.

## Tools/Technologies Mentioned

- **Graphify** — Knowledge graph generator for codebases (GitHub repo, open source)
- **Claude Code** — Primary coding agent that Graphify enhances
- **Hermes Agent** — Agent framework connected to the operating system
- **OpenCor** — Mentioned as part of the cross-device connectivity stack
- **Anti-Gravity** — Referenced as part of the agent ecosystem
- **Codex** — OpenAI Codex, also integrated into the OS

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

The Graphify pattern directly parallels the email-to-gbrain / Company Memory OS architecture:

| Graphify Pattern | gbrain/Company Memory OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Knowledge graph of code files | Knowledge graph of people, companies, decisions |
| "God nodes" = key files | "God nodes" = key relationships/deals |
| Query: "what depends on X?" | Query: "what was decided about Project X?" |
| Claude Code integration | Hermes Agent + gbrain integration |
| Visual dashboard | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| Compound context across sessions | Compound institutional memory across emails |

The video validates the architecture sayf has already built: knowledge graph + agentic layer + visual dashboard + autonomous pipeline. It's a compelling analogy for client pitches — "what Graphify does for code, Company Memory OS does for your business."

## Chapter Index

- 00:00 — Chapter 1: Graphify Solves Claude's Problem (hook + promise)
- 00:46 — Chapter 2: What Graphify Actually Does (the map metaphor)
- 01:21 — Chapter 3: How The Map Works (clustering, god nodes, facts vs guesses)
- 02:10 — Chapter 4: Why Maps Save Tokens (orientation, blast radius, re-reading tax)
- 03:17 — Chapter 5: Install And Set Up (clone repo, Claude Code integration)
- 04:38 — Chapter 6: See The Knowledge Graph (interactive visualization)
- 05:04 — Chapter 7: Query Any Repo Live (prompt example, cost comparison)
- 05:43 — Chapter 8: Take It Further (agentic operating system intro)
- 06:07 — Chapter 9: Inside The Operating System (dashboard, Hermes integration)
- 07:32 — Chapter 10: Import Any GitHub Repo (one-click ingestion)
- 08:07 — Chapter 11: Chat To Your Projects (conversational interface)
- 09:04 — Chapter 12: Connect Everything Together (shared brain architecture)
- 10:04 — Chapter 13: Visual Interface Wins (closing argument)

---

*Raw transcript extracted from YouTube timestamps provided by user (VPS IP blocked by YouTube, transcript retrieved via user-supplied text).*

**Date:** 2026-06-05
**Attendees:** [TJ van der Molen](people/tj-vandermolen), sayf

## About TJ

- **Role:** CEO & ECHA Specialist
- **Company:** [Passend](companies/passend)
- **Email:** t.vandermolen@passend.my
- **Website:** www.passend.my
- **Phone:** +6016 434 8614

## Summary

Met with [TJ van der Molen](people/tj-vandermolen), CEO and ECHA Specialist at [Passend](companies/passend). Discussed his business progress, partnerships, and growth plans. Explored how Maloha Coast can support Passend's development needs.

## Three Projects Discussed

### 1. Database + Frontend - Project-Based Learning Showcase

- **Primary users:** Admin & staff
- **Purpose:** Maintain chronological records of student projects & learning outcomes
- **Functionality:** Distribute updates to students, parents, and community via portal/newsletters/social media
- **Feature consideration:** Facial recognition/computer vision for obtaining parental consent before publishing student content

### 2. Internal Tool - Reporting Automation

- **Primary users:** Admin & staff recording behavioral observations for assessment
- **Challenge:** Excessive paperwork distracts teachers/instructors from effective student engagement
- **Solution:** Implement rapid data input tools with database storage and report generation
- **Quality control:** Staff verification system to validate AI/OCR data imports

### 3. SaaS Platform - Counselor Teaching & Learning

- **Primary users:** Counselors/teaching staff accessing Passend materials
- **Business model:** Freemium to subscription conversion
- **Tier structure:**
  - Level 1: Course access
  - Level 2: Curriculum sharing with colleagues
  - Level 3: Passend-hosted events
  - Certification available at all levels

## Next Steps

- Recommended a Requirements Engineering Workshop to develop detailed user stories and interaction requirements
- Workshop would produce a comprehensive task list usable by any development team
- Duration: 1 hour to full day depending on system complexity
- TJ to prioritize which of the three systems to pursue first

## Commercial

- Services offered through Maloha Coast Sdn Bhd
- sayf's calendar relatively open until May, after which contract work begins
- Requirements Engineering Workshop rates: MYR 100/hour or MYR 800/full day

[Source: User, 2026-06-05]

```
Hi TJ,

Thank you for meeting with me today. I thoroughly enjoyed learning about your business progress and partnerships.

I'm excited about your growth plans and exploring how I can support Passend. Below is a summary of the projects we discussed:

1. Database + Frontend - Project-Based Learning Showcase
   - Primary users: Admin & staff

   - Functionality: Distribute updates to students, parents, and community via portal/newsletters/social media

2. Internal Tool - Reporting Automation
   - Primary users: Admin & staff recording behavioral observations for assessment
   - Challenge: Excessive paperwork distracts teachers/instructors from effective student engagement
   - Solution: Implement rapid data input tools with database storage and report generation
   - Quality control: Staff verification system to validate AI/OCR data imports

3. SaaS Platform - Counselor Teaching & Learning
   - Primary users: Counselors/teaching staff accessing Passend materials
   - Business model: Freemium to subscription conversion
   - Tier structure:
     - Level 1: Course access
     - Level 2: Curriculum sharing with colleagues

Please feel free to correct any misunderstandings or add details I may have missed.

For next steps, I recommend a Requirements Engineering Workshop to develop detailed user stories and specific interaction requirements. This workshop would produce a comprehensive task list that any development team, including ours, could use to build your MVP. The documentation would cover all necessary frontend and backend components.

Workshop duration typically ranges from one hour to a full day depending on system complexity. You might consider prioritizing which of the three systems you'd like to pursue first.

These services are offered through my company, Maloha Coast Sdn Bhd. My calendar is relatively open until May, after which I'll begin contract work for several months. For transparency, our Requirements Engineering Workshop rates are MYR 100 per hour or MYR 800 for a full day.

Looking forward to potentially working together.
```

[[HumanLayer]] is opening up to everyone. Previously waitlist-only, the Agentic IDE and collaboration platform is now publicly available.

From [[Dex Horthy]] (dexter@hey.humanlayer.dev) and Kyle — the team behind the open-source Research, Plan, Implement (RPI) framework now running inside Fortune 500s (Block, Uber).

## What HumanLayer Is

An Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and building blocks for your software factory — where engineering teams design complex systems with their AI agents before generating a single line of code. It lets engineers solving hard problems in complex codebases:

- Move 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC (not just the coding part)
- Maintain rigorous standards for system architecture and program design

## Core Idea

Don't outsource the thinking. Great teams have run design reviews for decades because there's enormous leverage in collaborating on system design before writing code. HumanLayer brings that missing piece of the software factory into one place.

Bring your own subscription — plug in Claude, Codex, and other AI subscriptions or API keys. No separate per-token bill.

pages.title: Maloha Coast Navigator 2026 06 15
pages.body:
# The Maloha Coast Navigator

*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

---

## Client Spotlight

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

---

## What We're Reading

- [[Claude Code 10x Skill]] — The skill pattern that changed everything
- [[Second Brain Architecture]] — How we structure knowledge for SMBs

---

## Upcoming

pages.title: How provider data policies work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter newsletter to info@malohacoast.com

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

## What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train** — Account-wide toggle in privacy settings for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request** — Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering in provider routing docs.
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers** — Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## EU Routing

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via the https://eu.openrouter.ai base URL.

# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** email from sayf (forwarded from Neo Kim / System Design One)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2580f5fe7903
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:37:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

## Summary

System Design Newsletter #154 by Neo Kim and guest author Paul Hoekstra. A comprehensive framework of 30 core agentic engineering concepts organized into six layers: Foundations, Configuration, Capability, Orchestration, Guardrails, and Observability.

## Key Content

30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

By Neo Kim and Paul Hoekstra
Jun 17, 2026

How do you keep up with agentic engineering when a new tool ships every week? You learn the ideas behind the tools, and let the tools come and go.

By the end of this newsletter, you can read any agent post or news drop and see which of these ideas it actually covers, instead of feeling behind every time something new ships.

Here's what's inside:

Foundations
1. What is an agent? - An LLM that runs in a loop, has access to tools, and decides what to do next. Coding is the most obvious use case. Use an agent when the task is open-ended and the next step depends on the result of the last one.
2. Execution model - Three steps repeated: think, act, observe. Two variations: parallelism and blocking vs non-blocking.
3. Agent state - Context window vs everything outside it. Files are usually the right default for state.
4. Common agent patterns - Planner/executor, router/specialist, map-reduce parallelism.

Configuration Layer
5. Agent config files - Project-level markdown (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md) telling the agent your standards and limits.
6. Reusable workflow files - On-demand markdown files with YAML frontmatter (skills, Cursor rules).
7. Workflow frameworks - Superpowers, Get Shit Done, Compound Engineering.
8. Prompt caching - Stores stable prefix of conversation. Changes the economics of long config files.
9. Context rot - Model performance drops as context window fills up.

Capability Layer
10. Live documentation - The agent uses tools to fetch live docs (web_fetch, MCP).
11. AI-native search - Semantic search as a tool.
12. Knowledge graphing - Agents that build and update graphs.
13. Long-term memory - Survives across sessions.

Guest author: Paul Hoekstra, data engineer at Digital Power, writes Paul's Pipeline newsletter.

pages.title: 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** System Design Newsletter (Neo Kim & Paul Hoekstra)
**Published:** Jun 17, 2026
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:04:29+08:00

#154: What an agent is, how to configure one, when to use multiple, and how to keep them from doing damage.

*Guest author: Paul Hoekstra — data engineer at Digital Power, data and AI consultancy; writes Paul's Pipeline.*

## Foundations

### 1. What is an agent?
An agent is an LLM that runs in a loop, has access to tools, and decides what to do next. Instead of a single answer to a prompt, the model produces a chain of actions, each building on the last.

Use an agent when: task is open-ended, the next step depends on the last, you cannot script the path in advance. Skip the agent when a single prompt or plain script will do.

### 2. Execution model (Think-Act-Observe / ReAct)
Three steps, repeated: (1) model thinks — reads current state and decides next action; (2) acts — emits a tool call, harness validates and runs it; (3) observes — tool result joins conversation as context. The loop replaces upfront prediction with feedback, allowing the agent to recover from its own mistakes.

Two variations: parallelism (multiple tool calls in one act step) and blocking vs non-blocking (sync vs async).

### 3. Agent state
Two senses: (1) workflow progress — which step is the agent on; (2) everything the agent knows at any moment. Breaks into: context window (everything the model sees, hard ceiling) and everything outside it (files, DB, memory it fetches on demand). Files are the right default — diffable, versionable, easy to edit. Subagents get a fresh context window, parent passes only what's needed.

### 4. Common agent patterns
- **Planner/executor**: one plans, another executes. Keeps each agent in a suitable context shape.
- **Router/specialist**: router dispatches to specialists with tighter prompts and smaller toolsets. Cheaper, easier to reason about.
- **Map-reduce parallelism**: fan-out across subagents, merge results. Cost bounded by longest subagent.
- These combine in real workflows: planner spawns router → router fans out map-reduce → merged result comes back.

## Configuration Layer

### 5. Agent config files
Project-level Markdown loaded at session start (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md). The agent reads hard rules before writing code. Useful configs are short and specific: hard limits, actual tooling stack, behavioral rules. Keep under 100 lines.

### 6. Reusable workflow files (skills)
Markdown with YAML frontmatter — name, description, optional globs. Loaded on demand. SkillsBench showed Claude Haiku at 27.7% with human-curated skills beats Opus at 22.0% without them. Model-written skills saw gains disappear — generic AI-generated boilerplate makes things worse.

### 7. Workflow frameworks
Chains of skills, hooks, and slash commands wired into repeatable processes. Examples: Superpowers, Get Shit Done, Compound Engineering. Shared thread: understand what you're building, tell it how to work, check the result.

### 8. Prompt caching
Stores the stable prefix so the model doesn't re-read it every turn. First call pays a small premium to write the cache; later turns read at a discount. Cache TTL ranges from minutes to an hour depending on provider. Without caching you'd think carefully about config file size; with it, the cost is a rounding error.

### 9. Context rot
Model performance drops as context fills up. GPT-5.5: 98.1% on short documents → 74.0% at 1M tokens. Claude Opus 4.7: 59.2% at 256K → 32.2% at 1M. The model has to look past each token to focus on what matters.

## Key Insights
- Learn the ideas behind the tools, not the tools themselves — the pace of change won't slow down.
- Skills/benchmark data proves that good instructions beat a better model.
- Context rot is the hard constraint that all agentic workflows must design around.

pages.title: 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply (#154)
pages.body:
# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply (#154)

**Source:** email via System Design One (Substack)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2580f5fe7903
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf to Maloha Coast, 2026-06-20

By Neo Kim and guest author Paul Hoekstra (data engineer at Digital Power, writes Paul's Pipeline newsletter). June 17, 2026.

**Thesis:** Learn the ideas behind the tools, let the tools come and go. Beneath the noise, it's the same few ideas every time.

## Foundations

### 1. What is an agent?
An LLM that runs in a loop, has access to tools, and decides what to do next. Produces a chain of actions where each builds on the last.

**Use an agent when:** task is open-ended, the next step depends on the last result, you cannot script the path in advance.
**Skip the agent when:** a single prompt or plain script will do.

### 2. Execution model (ReAct / Think-Act-Observe)
Three steps repeated: **Think** (read state, decide) → **Act** (emit tool call, harness validates and runs it) → **Observe** (result joins conversation as new context).
- This is why agents can recover from their own mistakes.
- Two variations: Parallelism (multiple tool calls per act step) and Blocking vs non-blocking.

### 3. Agent state
Two halves: **Context window** (everything model can see, hard ceiling, gone when session ends) vs **Everything outside it** (files, DB, memory, knowledge).
- Files are usually the right default (diffable, versionable).
- Two agents reading same file = fine. Two writing = race condition.

### 4. Common agent patterns
- **Planner/Executor:** One plans, another executes. Keeps context shapes separate.
- **Router/Specialist:** Router dispatches to specialists with tighter prompts and smaller toolsets.
- **Map-reduce parallelism:** Fan-out across subagents, merge results. Cost bounded by longest individual subagent.
- **Combinations:** Real workflows combine all three.

## Configuration Layer

### 5. Agent config files
Project-level markdown (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md) loaded at session start. Short, specific, under 100 lines. Treated as code, not documentation.

### 6. Reusable workflow files
On-demand skills (e.g. .claude/skills/). YAML frontmatter controls when they load. SkillsBench showed Haiku + skills beat Opus without them (27.7% vs 22.0%).

### 7. Workflow frameworks
Superpowers, Get Shit Done, Compound Engineering — curated skill libraries with processes for planning, TDD, debugging, review.

### 8. Prompt caching
Stores stable prefix so model doesn't re-read every turn. First call pays a premium to write cache, later turns read at discount. TTL depends on provider.

### 9. Context rot
Model performance drops as context fills up. Even latest models show this: GPT-5.5 drops from 98.1% (short docs) to 74.0% (1M tokens). Claude Opus 4.7 drops from 59.2% (256K) to 32.2% (1M).

pages.title: The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers (#155)
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers (#155)

**Source:** email via System Design One (Substack)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee23d6478fc08e
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf to Maloha Coast, 2026-06-20

By Neo Kim and Ashish Prajapati. June 19, 2026.

**Thesis:** The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models — they're coming from better infrastructure.

## Mental Model: AI Data Center as Factory

- AI Data Center = Factory
- Data = Raw material
- GPUs = Machines on the factory floor
- Network = Internal transport system
- Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
- Software = Assembly line logic
- Output = Trained intelligence

## Three Physical Constraints

1. **Power:** A single server rack of GPUs can draw 80-120kW — roughly 40 homes at full load. Limited by how much electricity the facility can receive and distribute.

2. **Cooling:** More power = more heat. Modern data centers adopt liquid cooling — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs and CPUs. Enables higher rack densities and lower cooling power consumption.

3. **Space:** Even with unlimited chips and power, you need racks and floor space. Scaling AI is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined.

## Compute: GPU vs CPU vs DPU

- **CPU** = manager (decision-making, complex varied tasks)
- **GPU** = factory workers (thousands of simpler cores, mass parallel matrix ops)
- **DPU** = logistics (networking, storage I/O, security — offloads from GPU/CPU)

GPUs dominate AI because AI training repeatedly performs matrix multiplications billions of times — exactly what GPUs are designed for.

## Memory & Storage Hierarchy

The real bottleneck: GPUs don't fail because they're slow — they fail because they're starved for data.

| Tier | Tech | Speed | Capacity |
|------|------|-------|----------|
| GPU Memory | HBM | Blazing fast | Tens of GB |
| CPU RAM | DDR | Fast | Hundreds of GB |
| Local Storage | NVMe SSD | Moderate | TB+ |
| Network/Object | S3 | Slowest | Unlimited |

In practice: Hot (NVMe SSDs for active datasets), Warm (parallel file systems like Lustre, GPFS), Cold (object storage like S3).

## Networking — The Hidden Bottleneck

- **Leaf-spine architecture:** Every GPU server reaches every other in at most 2 hops
- **Speed hierarchy:** NVLink (intra-node, fastest) > InfiniBand/RoCE (inter-node) > Ethernet (general-purpose)
- **Amdahl's Law:** Adding more GPUs eventually stops helping due to communication overhead

**Smart data movement techniques:** RDMA (direct memory-to-memory), GPUDirect (GPU talks to storage/network directly bypassing CPU), zero-copy pipelines.

**Goal:** Make thousands of GPUs behave like one giant unified processor.

pages.title: The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

**Source:** email from Sayf Codes, forwarded from Neo Kim (System Design One / Substack)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee23d6478fc08e
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded systemdesignone@substack.com → sayfcodes@gmail.com → info@malohacoast.com

Originally from: The System Design Newsletter by Neo Kim. Featuring guest author Ashish Prajapati (AI infrastructure specialist).

## Summary

A deep dive into the physical and architectural reality of AI data centers. Covers why AI infrastructure is as much a physics problem as a software one — power, cooling, compute, memory hierarchy, and networking at scale.

## Key Ideas

### Mental Model: AI Data Center as a Factory
- AI Data Center = Factory
- Data = Raw material
- GPUs = Machines on the factory floor
- Network = Internal transport system
- Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
- Software = Assembly line logic
- Output = Trained intelligence

### Three Physical Constraints
1. **Power**: Single server rack of GPUs draws 80-120 kW (~40 homes at full load). Limited by what the facility can physically receive and distribute.
2. **Cooling**: More power = more heat. Liquid cooling is replacing air cooling — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs/CPUs. Enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power consumption.
3. **Space**: Unlimited chips + unlimited power still needs racks and floor space. AI scaling is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined.

### Compute Architecture
- **CPU** = manager (complex, varied, sequential tasks; coordinates)
- **GPU** = factory workers (thousands of simple cores for parallel matrix multiplication)
- **DPU** = logistics (handles networking, storage I/O, security — frees GPU/CPU)

AI training is repeatedly performing matrix multiplications billions of times — GPUs are designed for this.

### Memory Hierarchy
- **GPU Memory (HBM)**: Blazingly fast, tiny (tens of GB) — GPU's working memory
- **CPU RAM**: Hundreds of GB, slower than HBM
- **SSD/NVMe**: Enormous capacity, slower still
- **Network/Object Storage**: Nearly unlimited, slowest link

The bottleneck: GPUs don't fail because they're slow — they fail because they're starved for data. AI performance = how fast data can be fed into the GPU, not how fast the GPU computes.

### Networking at Scale
- Modern AI clusters use leaf-spine architecture: every GPU server reaches every other in at most 2 hops
- **NVLink**: fastest path, intra-node GPU communication
- **InfiniBand/RoCE**: inter-node, "bullet train" of the data center
- **Ethernet**: general-purpose but not designed for AI's extreme demands
- **Amdahl's Law**: adding more GPUs eventually stops helping because communication overhead grows

### Smarter Data Movement
- **RDMA**: direct memory-to-memory data transfers between servers
- **GPUDirect**: GPUs read/write to storage/network directly, bypassing CPU
- Zero-copy pipelines: data takes shortest path with no redundant copying

### About the Guest Author
- **Ashish Prajapati**: AI infrastructure specialist and technology educator
- Training programs reached 39,000+ learners worldwide
- Runs BeSA (Become a Solutions Architect) mentoring initiative — helped 23,000+ professionals
- Offers NCA-AIIO bootcamp (NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and Operations)

## Relevance for Maloha Coast

- Understanding AI infrastructure informs deployment decisions for Hermes Agent hosting
- GPU topology knowledge relevant if running local models
- Ashish Prajapati's BeSA program may be a useful resource for the team
- The factory mental model is useful for explaining AI infrastructure to clients

pages.title: AI Garage Weekly — June 19, 2026
pages.body:
# AI Garage Weekly — June 19, 2026

**Source:** email via AI Garage Weekly (Beehiiv)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2c24d5819622
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf to Maloha Coast, 2026-06-20

By Tonbi. Topics: Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI.

## Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel
Tonbi built a Hermes Agent integration inside Excel — getting a chat window right in Excel plus custom functions powered by Hermes. Open source GitHub repo available.

## AI News & Model Releases

### Claude Fable 5 banned
Anthropic removed Fable 5 access due to a new US government directive. Tonbi's take: these models are so powerful the government won't let private companies have that capability unchecked. Highlights the need for local and decentralized AI.

### GLM-5.2 released
New open weights model. Feels only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT-5.5 — one generation behind. Incredible for open source, though requires serious hardware to run locally without quality degradation.

## Research & Experiments

### DR-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion
(https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885)

A retrieval/workspace pattern that goes beyond RAG. In standard DCI, the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus. Problem: breaks when corpus is huge. DR-DCI hides the huge corpus behind a retriever; agent calls `pull(query, topK)` to bring relevant documents into a local workspace, then uses normal file tools on that smaller workspace.

Result: RAG-like recall with filesystem agency from DCI. Increased accuracy, improved wall time and costs.

## Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

### Agent Wikis
Nearly 10k visitors on release day. Refining autonomous pipeline for maintaining wikis — loop so missed topics get researched and autonomously added. Exploring premium wikis with Stripe integration and possibly small ads with agentic ad pipeline.

> Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community. One showed why local AI is so important, the other showed that open weights models can approach the top closed frontier models. We just need to lower the hardware barrier.

## Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel

[[Tonbi]]'s wife was using Claude in Excel, and he wondered if he could do it with [[Hermes Agent]]. After a false start with an overly complicated method, he found a simpler approach. The end result: a chat window inside Excel plus custom functions with Hermes. The video didn't go viral but got nice comments. He plans more integrations like this.

[How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.maopPdr3OQftpzkXs5OR4-tDkO_ZYupC2QTCbId7IkHkiJxZzzIEuleA0Zxl07q_mIlFIjX1d-qo0fTV8ktpwzR6AzV7NOC9JaoERjTgGRKKpgQr8t9M6Ax6eRdxc2VS1KSZt9LH0au-x505YOJCIHODQTRnFuG4RF0Fca33iwrdB49JxH-Wpzn-QIBG9tCOiNcW0-lZMJIx8ziuwfUR4WLoyIC_B8CQQdw3BksEQ74jMfWREhLH2w5wWjK9POqQ/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h2/h001.Uo1z3BXiQOSpNs0ZoYyWZCZTVSRrzmTQWvnIe6dPxT8)

## AI News & Model Releases

**Claude Fable 5 banned.** [[Anthropic]] removed Fable 5 access due to a new US government directive. [[Tonbi]] wasn't surprised — predicted this direction. Thinks it's some weird Anthropic/admin negotiating in public and will get cleared up eventually, but highlights the need for local and decentralized AI.

**GLM-5.2 released.** Open weights model. [[Tonbi]] gave it tasks in a first look video and has been using it. Feels "only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind." Incredible for an open source model, though most people won't be able to run it locally without serious hardware.

## Research & Experiments

**DR-DCI (Dynamic Retrieval-Direct Corpus Interaction).** Paper at arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885. Describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern combining RAG recall with filesystem agency. Key insight: hide huge corpus behind a retriever, let agent call `pull(query, topK)` to bring relevant docs into a local workspace, then use normal file tools. Increases accuracy while improving wall time and costs. [[Tonbi]] sees clear applications for [[Agent Wikis]].

## Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

**[[Agent Wikis]]:** First release day got nearly 10k visitors (humans and agents), now doing 5k-7k daily. [[Tonbi]] created a loop for missed topics to be autonomously researched and added to wikis. Considering premium wikis with Stripe integration and an autonomous ad pipeline where the agent develops, releases, and optimizes ads.

pages.body:
# AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026 From: Sayf Codes To: Maloha Coast Subject: Fwd: AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026 Date: Sat, 20 Jun 2026 10:00:33 +0800 Delivered-To: info@malohacoast.com ---------- Forwarded message --------- From: AI Garage Weekly Date: Sat, 20 Jun 2026, 01:31 Subject: AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026 To: sayfcodes@gmail.com Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026 Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI < Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community I would say. One showed why local AI is so important, the other showed that it was actually possible to have open weights models that approach the top closed frontier models. We just need to lower the hardware barrier somehow and I think local AI will really explode. **please consider joining Team Garage < Thank you!* *🎬 Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel.* So my wife was talking about using Claude in Excel for her work, and I had the thought: Could I do that with Hermes Agent? I hadn’t heard of that type of integration, I wasn’t even sure if it was possible, but I started to research a way to do it. The first attempt I actually recorded a full video using a much more complicated, much more frustrating technique. I got to the end of it and asked my agent: Isn’t there an easier way to do this? And after some extra research, they said yes! "YOU SHOULD'VE TOLD ME THAT TO BEGIN WITH” I screamed in my head, not wanting to upset my agent too much (lol). In the end that was the method I ended up recording for this video, it does require some prep work to start up, but the end result is pretty cool. You get a chat window right in Excel, plus you can create custom functions with your Hermes. I was excited for this video and while I got a lot of nice comments on it, it didn’t go super viral as I had hoped (lol). I would still like to try more integrations like this, I think this would be genuinely useful for a lot of people. How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel (And Made It a Custom Function) < If you want to try it out yourself, check out my open source github. < 📡* AI News & Model Releases* *Claude Fable 5 banned. *So last Friday after I had sent out my newsletter, the big news came out that Anthropic was removing Fable 5 access due to a new directive from the US government. It was really surprising news, a lot of people were really blown away by Fable and disappointed by this move. I was disappointed too, but I’ve been convinced this was the direction we were headed anyway. These models are so powerful, the government isn’t going to let private companies and individuals have power like that. They’ll want to control it. I think this specific case is just some weird Anthropic and the current administration negotiating in public and it will get cleared up eventually, but it still highlights the need for local and decentralized AI moving forward. You can’t let all that power be handed to only the current administration, or 1-2 companies, people should have access to these tools. *GLM-5.2 released.* I’ve been impressed with GLM models before, so when I heard about this new open weights model release, I was excited to try. It was really impressive! I gave it some tasks in my first look video < and I’ve been using it a bit since then, and it does feel like only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind. This is still an incredible feat for an open source model, and it does make me think that we will get genuine frontier-level open source models sooner than we think. Now of course most people won’t be able to run them locally, it would take some serious hardware to be able to run it that isn’t super slow and quality-degraded, but it just shows what is possible for the future. 📄* Research & Experiments* *Dr-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion * (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885 < With Agent Wikis, one of the big things I’ve been researching is retrieval, how agents are able to collect and process information. I wanted to see if I could find a way to go beyond RAG, or enhance RAG in some way. This paper caught my eye. It describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern that could be effective for Agent Wikis, or having any agent handle any large corpus. With normal RAG, you have the agent ask a retriever, get snippets, and then answer from those snippets. With DCI, and earlier version of this method, the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus, and can use tools like find, read, compare files, inspect evidence. The problem with this is that it breaks when the corpus is huge. Search millions of files directly is slow and noisy. So with DR-DCI, that huge corpus is hidden behind a retriever, but the agent can call pull(query, topK), to bring relevant documents into a local workspace, then use normal file tools on that smaller workspace. So it actually gives you RAG-like recall with filesystem agency from DCI. They found that using this method increased accuracy as well as significantly improved wall time and costs. So the applications for Agent Wikis is pretty clear, I experimented with RAG over the wikis and found that to be one the best methods, but some of these wikis are quite large, so it could become challenging. Since these are specifically agent focused, I think having this smaller workspace to pull relevant chunks out could be useful, because I imagine people are using this knowledge not for just one-off questions, but for ongoing project work. Still need to figure out how or if I can even do this, but I thought it was interesting. 💭* Random Brainstorming & Project Progress* *Agents Wikis: *Nice release for Agent Wikis < people seemed to really like it and the first release day got nearly 10k visitors on the site (humans and agents), and we’ve been doing 5k-7k since then. Very large improvement from the last time I released a project before my YouTube channel (I was happy with 10 visitors, lol). I’ve been refining the pipeline to maintaining these wikis, trying to make it as autonomous as possible without ruining everything. The one thing I did was to create a loop so that missed topics will get researched and then autonomously added to the wikis. This will ensure that the wikis actually answer the questions that people have on these topics (without any extra work from me). Yesterday’s video showed this in action. I’ve been exploring other ways to allow agents to maintain and enhance the product. I think once I’m ready to do premium wikis with the stripe integration, that would be a good place for it. I may also explore doing some small ads after that to see how those can drive traffic. I would love to do an advertising pipeline where the agent develops all the ad material (so I can be lazy), releases the ads, then analyzes the results and hones the ads based on that. I’ve heard of people doing this, but that would be an amazing loop to have. The ads themselves would cost money though, so I’ll need the site to be profitable before I try that. *AI Garage Weekly publishes every Friday.* *Tonbi’s AI Garage < Share on:Facebook < |Threads < |X < |LinkedIn < [image: AI Garage Weekly] © 2026 AI Garage Weekly 228 Park Ave S, #29976, New York, New York 10003, United States < [image: beehiiv logo]Powered by beehiiv < Terms of Service <

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: AI Garage Weekly <ai-garage-weekly@mail.beehiiv.com>
Date: Sat, 20 Jun 2026, 01:31
Subject: AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026
To: sayfcodes@gmail.com <sayfcodes@gmail.com>


Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI                                                                                                                                                                                                         

AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026

Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community I would say. One showed why local AI is so important, the other showed that it was actually possible to have open weights models that approach the top closed frontier models. We just need to lower the hardware barrier somehow and I think local AI will really explode.

If you want to support my work and get more of my content, please consider joining Team Garage. Thank you!

 

🎬 Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel. So my wife was talking about using Claude in Excel for her work, and I had the thought: Could I do that with Hermes Agent? I hadn’t heard of that type of integration, I wasn’t even sure if it was possible, but I started to research a way to do it. The first attempt I actually recorded a full video using a much more complicated, much more frustrating technique. I got to the end of it and asked my agent: Isn’t there an easier way to do this? And after some extra research, they said yes! "YOU SHOULD'VE TOLD ME THAT TO BEGIN WITH” I screamed in my head, not wanting to upset my agent too much (lol). In the end that was the method I ended up recording for this video, it does require some prep work to start up, but the end result is pretty cool. You get a chat window right in Excel, plus you can create custom functions with your Hermes. I was excited for this video and while I got a lot of nice comments on it, it didn’t go super viral as I had hoped (lol). I would still like to try more integrations like this, I think this would be genuinely useful for a lot of people.

How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel (And Made It a Custom Function)

If you want to try it out yourself, check out my open source github.

 

📡 AI News & Model Releases

Claude Fable 5 banned. So last Friday after I had sent out my newsletter, the big news came out that Anthropic was removing Fable 5 access due to a new directive from the US government. It was really surprising news, a lot of people were really blown away by Fable and disappointed by this move. I was disappointed too, but I’ve been convinced this was the direction we were headed anyway. These models are so powerful, the government isn’t going to let private companies and individuals have power like that. They’ll want to control it. I think this specific case is just some weird Anthropic and the current administration negotiating in public and it will get cleared up eventually, but it still highlights the need for local and decentralized AI moving forward. You can’t let all that power be handed to only the current administration, or 1-2 companies, people should have access to these tools.

GLM-5.2 released. I’ve been impressed with GLM models before, so when I heard about this new open weights model release, I was excited to try. It was really impressive! I gave it some tasks in my first look video and I’ve been using it a bit since then, and it does feel like only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind. This is still an incredible feat for an open source model, and it does make me think that we will get genuine frontier-level open source models sooner than we think. Now of course most people won’t be able to run them locally, it would take some serious hardware to be able to run it that isn’t super slow and quality-degraded, but it just shows what is possible for the future.

 

📄 Research & Experiments

Dr-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885) With Agent Wikis, one of the big things I’ve been researching is retrieval, how agents are able to collect and process information. I wanted to see if I could find a way to go beyond RAG, or enhance RAG in some way. This paper caught my eye. It describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern that could be effective for Agent Wikis, or having any agent handle any large corpus.

With normal RAG, you have the agent ask a retriever, get snippets, and then answer from those snippets. With DCI, and earlier version of this method, the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus, and can use tools like find, read, compare files, inspect evidence. The problem with this is that it breaks when the corpus is huge. Search millions of files directly is slow and noisy. So with DR-DCI, that huge corpus is hidden behind a retriever, but the agent can call pull(query, topK), to bring relevant documents into a local workspace, then use normal file tools on that smaller workspace. So it actually gives you RAG-like recall with filesystem agency from DCI. They found that using this method increased accuracy as well as significantly improved wall time and costs.

So the applications for Agent Wikis is pretty clear, I experimented with RAG over the wikis and found that to be one the best methods, but some of these wikis are quite large, so it could become challenging. Since these are specifically agent focused, I think having this smaller workspace to pull relevant chunks out could be useful, because I imagine people are using this knowledge not for just one-off questions, but for ongoing project work. Still need to figure out how or if I can even do this, but I thought it was interesting.

 

 💭 Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

Agents Wikis: Nice release for Agent Wikis, people seemed to really like it and the first release day got nearly 10k visitors on the site (humans and agents), and we’ve been doing 5k-7k since then. Very large improvement from the last time I released a project before my YouTube channel (I was happy with 10 visitors, lol). I’ve been refining the pipeline to maintaining these wikis, trying to make it as autonomous as possible without ruining everything. The one thing I did was to create a loop so that missed topics will get researched and then autonomously added to the wikis. This will ensure that the wikis actually answer the questions that people have on these topics (without any extra work from me). Yesterday’s video showed this in action.

I’ve been exploring other ways to allow agents to maintain and enhance the product. I think once I’m ready to do premium wikis with the stripe integration, that would be a good place for it. I may also explore doing some small ads after that to see how those can drive traffic. I would love to do an advertising pipeline where the agent develops all the ad material (so I can be lazy), releases the ads, then analyzes the results and hones the ads based on that. I’ve heard of people doing this, but that would be an amazing loop to have. The ads themselves would cost money though, so I’ll need the site to be profitable before I try that.

 

AI Garage Weekly publishes every Friday.

Tonbi’s AI Garage

Share on:Facebook|Threads|X|LinkedIn

 

© 2026 AI Garage Weekly

228 Park Ave S, #29976, New York, New York 10003, United States
