# Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

**Source:** email from sayf (forwarded from Nate Herk / AI Automation Society)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2dfa87f86548
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:37:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

## Summary

Newsletter from AI Automation Society (Nate Herk) covering three topics: a framework for building Claude second brains across five levels, a case study of automating a client's outbound email pipeline, and OpenAI Codex reaching 5M weekly users with banked rate limit resets.

## Key Content

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Sayf <sayfti@gmail.com>
Date: Sat, 20 Jun 2026, 10:25
Subject: Fwd: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained
To: Sayf Codes <sayfcodes@gmail.com>

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Nate Herk <nate@aiautomationsociety.ai>
Date: Sat, 20 Jun 2026, 06:05
Subject: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained
To: sayfti@gmail.com <sayfti@gmail.com>

A Claude second brain breakdown, a client pipeline automated, and Codex hits 5M users with a free bonus through June 24.

June 19, 2026
Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

A Claude second brain breakdown, a client pipeline automated, and Codex hits 5M users with a free bonus through June 24.

In This Issue

- Five levels of building a Claude second brain, from basic file search to fully autonomous recall.
- Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline and got their call center actually following up.
- OpenAI's Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, and there's a free bonus available through June 24 if you use it.

New Video: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most people are stuck at level one.

Level one is just finding a file by searching an exact word. Level five is a fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client relationships, and your IP better than you do. The difference between them isn't the tool. It's how the data is organized.

The insight that changes how you build: Start from the end. Before you organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of the basketball determines the size of the hoop. Jon shows his actual setup (a Herku project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not exact match) and full relationship mapping.

How this helps you: Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.

Winning Is All We Do
Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline from scratch

What happened: Robert's client had a call center calling leads, but nothing was following up by email. Sales managers emailed Robert their daily lead lists as Excel files. He built an automation that handles the rest: when the file lands in his inbox, it extracts the email, address, and name data, normalizes it, and uploads every contact directly into Instantly as a ready-to-run campaign. No manual cleanup. No copy-paste.

The opportunity for you: Businesses running call centers almost always have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone could fix.

In the News
Codex just hit 5 million weekly users. If you use it, there's a free bonus through June 24.

The news: OpenAI Codex has crossed 5 million weekly active users and launched two features alongside the milestone. One is permanent: banked rate limit resets. Instead of your weekly limit resetting on a fixed timer, you can now save resets and use them when you actually need them. The other runs through June 24: Plus and Pro users can invite up to three friends to try Codex. When a friend sends their first Codex message, you both get a bonus reset.

How it affects you: If you've been hitting Codex rate limits at the wrong moment mid-build, the banked reset system fixes that. You decide when to use your reset, not the clock.

pages.body:
# Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

**Source:** email from Nate Herk / AI Automation Society (forwarded by Sayf), 2026-06-20
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2dfa87f86548
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf via sayfti@gmail.com → sayfcodes@gmail.com

---

**June 19, 2026 — AI Automation Society Newsletter**

## In This Issue

- Five levels of building a Claude second brain, from basic file search to fully autonomous recall
- Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline and got their call center actually following up
- OpenAI's Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, with a free bonus through June 24

---

## Five Levels of a Claude Second Brain

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most people are stuck at level one.

Level one is just finding a file by searching an exact word. Level five is a fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client relationships, and your IP better than you do. The difference between them isn't the tool — it's how the data is organized.

**The insight that changes how you build:** Start from the end. Before you organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of the basketball determines the size of the hoop.

Jon shows his actual setup (a Herku project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not exact match) and full relationship mapping.

> **How this helps you:** Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.

---

## Client Win: Automated Outbound Email Pipeline

Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline from scratch. The client had a call center calling leads, but nothing was following up by email. Sales managers emailed Robert their daily lead lists as Excel files. He built an automation that:

1. When the file lands in his inbox, extracts email, address, and name data
2. Normalizes it (no "N123 MAIN STREET" showing up as a deliverable address)
3. Uploads every contact directly into Instantly as a ready-to-run campaign

No manual cleanup. No copy-paste. Just results.

> **The opportunity:** Businesses running call centers almost always have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone could fix.

---

## In the News: Codex Hits 5M Weekly Users

OpenAI Codex crossed 5 million weekly active users and launched two features:

1. **Banked rate limit resets (permanent)** — Instead of weekly limits resetting on a fixed timer, users can now save resets and use them when needed.
2. **Referral bonus (through June 24)** — Plus and Pro users can invite up to three friends; both get a bonus reset when a friend sends their first Codex message.

> **Signal:** Codex is growing through referral mechanics at 5 million weekly users. That's a tool people are actively building with and recommending.

---

## Related Entities

- [[Nate Herk]] — Author / AI Automation Society
- [[AI Automation Society]] — Newsletter publisher
- [[OpenAI Codex]] — Mentioned milestone
- [[Claude Second Brain]] — Topic of the main feature
- [[Herku]] — Jon's project setup tool
- [[Instantly]] — Email campaign platform used by Robert

pages.title: Humanlayer Launch 2026 06 19 Sayf
pages.body:
# HumanLayer // We're live — come build with us

**Source:** email from [[Dex Horthy]] (HumanLayer), forwarded by Sayf Codes, 2026-06-19
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edfd1054c17600
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20

[[HumanLayer]] has opened to the public — an Agentic IDE and collaboration platform for engineering teams.

## Summary

HumanLayer is an Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and software factory building blocks. The core idea: don't outsource the thinking — collaborate on system design before writing code, the way great engineering teams have run design reviews for decades.

## Key Points

- **Starter plan is free** for teams of fewer than three
- **BYO subscription** — bring your own Claude, Codex, and other AI subscriptions or API keys; no separate per-token bill
- Built on their open-source **Research, Plan, Implement (RPI)** framework, now running inside Fortune 500s like Block and Uber
- **Moves 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC** (not just coding)
- **Maintains rigorous standards** for system architecture and program design
- Launch posts on X (https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/2067286892786454855) and LinkedIn

## People Mentioned

- **[[Dex Horthy]]** (dexter@hey.humanlayer.dev, @dexhorthy on X) — co-founder of HumanLayer
- **[[Kyle]]** — co-founder of HumanLayer (alongside Dex)

## Companies Mentioned

- **[[HumanLayer]]** — the company launching the product
- **Block** — Fortune 500 user of their RPI framework
- **Uber** — Fortune 500 user of their RPI framework

# HumanLayer // We're Live — Come Build With Us

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edfd1054c17600
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf to Maloha Coast, 2026-06-19

From: dex at humanlayer <dexter@hey.humanlayer.dev>
Date: Thu, 18 Jun 2026

HumanLayer is an Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and building blocks for your software factory — where engineering teams design complex systems with their AI agents before generating a single line of code.

**Key concept:** "Don't outsource the thinking." Great teams have run design reviews for decades because there's enormous leverage in collaborating on system design before you write the code. HumanLayer brings that missing piece of the software factory into one place.

**Features:**
- Move 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC (not just the coding part)
- Maintain rigorous standards for system architecture and program design
- Bring your own subscription — plug in Claude, Codex, and other AI subscriptions
- No separate per-token bill
- Starter plan is free for teams of fewer than three

**Background:** Built by Dex and Kyle, who previously created the open-source Research, Plan, Implement (RPI) framework — now running inside Fortune 500s like Block and Uber.

**Launch links:**
- X: https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/2067286892786454855
- LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/dexterihorthy_at-humanlayer-were-on-a-mission-to-solve-ugcPost-7473052840899407872-Zb5U

pages.title: HumanLayer // We're live — come build with us
pages.body:
# HumanLayer Launch — Agentic IDE and Collaboration Platform

**Source:** email from Sayf Codes, forwarded from dex at HumanLayer
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edfd1054c17600
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded dex@hey.humanlayer.dev → sayfcodes@gmail.com → info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

HumanLayer launches publicly — an Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and software factory for engineering teams. Founded by Kyle and Dex, creators of the open-source RPI (Research, Plan, Implement) framework used inside Fortune 500s like Block and Uber.

## Key Features
- **Agentic IDE**: Design complex systems with AI agents before generating code
- **Collaboration platform**: Design reviews for system architecture before writing code
- **BYO subscription**: Plug in Claude, Codex, or other AI subscriptions/API keys — no separate per-token bill
- **Free Starter plan**: Free for teams of fewer than three
- **Claims**: 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC (not just coding)

## Core Philosophy
"Don't outsource the thinking." Great teams have run design reviews for decades — HumanLayer brings that missing piece of the software factory into one place with AI collaboration.

## Launch Links
- X: https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/2067286892786454855
- LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/dexterihorthy_at-humanlayer-were-on-a-mission-to-solve-ugcPost-7473052840899407872-Zb5U
- Launch blurb: `curl https://humanlayer.com/launch/blurb.md`

## Relevance for Maloha Coast
- The RPI framework (Research, Plan, Implement) is a known pattern for coding agents
- HumanLayer's "design before code" approach could be useful for Maloha Coast's agent architecture work
- Worth exploring as a tool for client-facing agent development
- Free tier available for small teams

pages.body:
# The Maloha Coast Navigator

*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

---

## Client Spotlight

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

---

## What We're Reading

- [[Claude Code 10x Skill]] — The skill pattern that changed everything
- [[Second Brain Architecture]] — How we structure knowledge for SMBs

---

## Upcoming

Next week: Pricing models that work for SMB AI consulting.

*— Sayf, Maloha Coast*

pages.title: Your coding agent + 300 models
pages.body:
Cursor, Cline, Continue, Claude Code. All work with one OpenRouter key.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

	

Hi there,

Coding agents are the fastest-growing use case on OpenRouter. One API key gives your agent access to every model, with automatic provider fallback if one goes down. You stop maintaining four provider accounts and one consolidated usage view replaces four separate dashboards.

See integrations ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/frameworks?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=b9_agents&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

	

The major agents already speak OpenRouter.

Cursor. Drop your OpenRouter key into Settings and pick any model from the dropdown. Switch between Claude, GPT, Gemini, and DeepSeek per task without leaving the editor.
Cline. Autonomous coding with auto-routing. The agent picks the right model per step, so planning runs on a thinking model and execution runs on something faster and cheaper.
Continue. Open-source, configurable. Point the base URL at OpenRouter and every model is available from the model picker.
Claude Code. Use OpenRouter as the backend to track team usage, set per-key rate limits, and control spend across models without touching Anthropic billing directly.

Why the multi-model swap matters for agents. Most coding agents are cheaper and faster when you route structured steps like file reads and diff writes to a small model, and reserve a frontier model for the part that actually writes code. The cost gap between a $0.15/M token small model and a $15/M token frontier model stacks up fast when the agent makes 50 calls per task.

Usage visibility. The activity dashboard shows every request with its model, cost, latency, and token counts. If an agent runs away, you see it inside minutes, not at the end of a billing cycle. Per-key rate limits stop the bleeding without disabling the whole integration.

openrouter.ai ( https://openrouter.ai?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Discord ( https://discord.com/invite/8TpuzNXQYr?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  X ( https://x.com/openrouter?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Docs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

pages.body:
#155: Why AI breakthroughs are really infrastructure breakthroughs
                                        
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                               

===

------------------------------
The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwiaWF0IjoxNzgxODYwNDAzLCJleHAiOjE3ODQ0NTI0MDMsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.YdmtNfofj9kNOvfhe0cEAH64YdVJ2yKsV0IJWCJV8Dw>#155:
Why AI breakthroughs are really infrastructure breakthroughs

Neo Kim <https://substack.com/@systemdesignone> and Ashish
<https://substack.com/@ashish903926>
Jun 19
∙
Preview
<https://substack.com/@systemdesignone> <https://substack.com/@ashish903926>

<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwicmVhY3Rpb24iOiLinaQiLCJpYXQiOjE3ODE4NjA0MDMsImV4cCI6MTc4NDQ1MjQwMywiaXNzIjoicHViLTE1MTE4NDUiLCJzdWIiOiJyZWFjdGlvbiJ9.tU2mTSEMMvD4mFFvWxkW1uoyHQCyo6Y9vYwyz_Y6Ejc&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=2k46ui>
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwiaWF0IjoxNzgxODYwNDAzLCJleHAiOjE3ODQ0NTI0MDMsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.YdmtNfofj9kNOvfhe0cEAH64YdVJ2yKsV0IJWCJV8Dw&r=2k46ui&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email>
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwiaWF0IjoxNzgxODYwNDAzLCJleHAiOjE3ODQ0NTI0MDMsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.YdmtNfofj9kNOvfhe0cEAH64YdVJ2yKsV0IJWCJV8Dw>

   -

   

------------------------------

When people talk about Artificial Intelligence (*AI*), they often refer to
models such as Generative Pre-trained Transformers (*GPTs*), Large Language
Models (*LLMs*), and neural networks.

It sounds like magic, software that *thinks*…

But here’s the uncomfortable truth:

The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models; they’re
coming from better infrastructure.

If AI were only about the models, there would be no need for huge,
warehouse-sized data centers packed with specialized hardware and powered
by massive amounts of electricity.

So what’s actually going on inside those buildings?

To make sense of everything that follows, we need a simple mental model.

Let’s find out one layer at a time…

Onward.
------------------------------
*Review the actual change, not the file list (Partner)
>> *Review your next PR with CodeRabbit Review Today

------------------------------
First Problem: Physics

*Before AI is a software problem, it’s a physics problem…*

------------------------------
Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage

Here’s something that surprises most people:

GPUs don’t fail because they’re slow. They fail because they’re
hungry—constantly starved for data.

<https://substack.com/redirect/e20c200f-be89-420c-bba9-c3fd72bb261e
Memory Hierarchy

Data doesn’t live in one place. It moves through a layered system, trading
speed for capacity:

   -

   GPU Memory (HBM²): Blazingly fast but tiny; usually tens of gigabytes.
   This is the GPU’s working memory.
   -

   CPU RAM: Larger (hundreds of gigabytes), but slower than HBM.
   -

   SSD/NVMe³ Storage: Enormous capacity at a lower cost, but still slower.
   -

   Network/Object Storage: Nearly unlimited, but the slowest link in the
   chain.

Every time the GPU needs data that isn’t already in its fast local memory,
it must wait... And a GPU sitting idle costs just as much money as one
doing useful work.

*Takeaway*

AI performance is not determined solely by how fast the GPU computes.
Instead, by how fast data can be fed into the GPU.

A faster chip with a slow data pipeline is like a faster car stuck in
traffic.
Tiered storage in practice

In real AI data centers, storage is organized into tiers that roughly map
to hot, warm, and cold data:

   -

   *Hot*: NVMe SSDs holding active training datasets and model checkpoints.
   -

   *Warm*: Parallel file systems⁴, such as Lustre, General Parallel File
   System (*GPFS*), provide high-speed shared access across many GPUs.
   -

   *Cold*: Object storage, such as Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon
   S3), for storing raw datasets and older checkpoints.

As models grow larger, a single GPU is no longer enough…

Techniques such as model sharding⁵ and distributed training⁶ distribute the
model across multiple GPUs and memory layers.

And once data starts moving across machines, a new bottleneck appears: *how
those machines communicate with each other.*

*Reminder: this is a teaser of the subscriber-only newsletter series,
exclusive to my golden members.*

When you upgrade, you’ll get:

   -

   *High-level architecture of real-world systems.*
   -

   Deep dive into how popular real-world systems actually work.
   -

   *How real-world systems handle scale, reliability, and performance.*

------------------------------
Networking: Bottleneck Nobody Talks About

When training a large AI model, you typically use thousands of GPUs working
together rather than a single GPU…

And those thousands of GPUs must constantly communicate with each other,
sharing what they’ve learned at every step…
<https://substack.com/redirect/416747cc-b41c-43c4-b795-c8e297cddfcd

Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is a second of lost
productivity and wasted investment.
Cluster topology and fabrics

Modern AI clusters typically use leaf-spine network architectures⁷: *a
design built for high throughput and low latency at scale*.

Every GPU server can reach every other GPU server in *at most ‘two’ hops*.

Within a node, GPUs talk over NVLink (intra-node fabric). Between nodes,
traffic flows over InfiniBand or RDMA over Converged Ethernet (*RoCE*), the
inter-node fabric⁸.

There’s also a clear speed hierarchy:

   -

   NVLink⁹: fastest path, a direct, private highway connecting GPUs within
   the same server.
   -

   InfiniBand/RoCE¹⁰: very high-speed interconnect between separate
   servers. Imagine it as the “bullet train” of the data center.
   -

   Ethernet: general-purpose, flexible, and ubiquitous, but not designed
   for AI’s most extreme bandwidth and latency demands.

Amdahl’s Law: Why More GPUs Aren’t Always Better

There’s a fundamental principle in distributed computing called *Amdahl’s
Law*.

In plain English: if parts of your system must wait for other parts, adding
more workers eventually stops helping.

In AI workloads, adding more GPUs increases computing power but also
increases communication overhead. As GPUs spend more time sharing and
synchronizing data, the bottleneck gradually shifts from computation to
communication.
Smarter data movement

Modern AI systems address this with several techniques:

   -

   Remote Direct Memory Access (*RDMA*): Provides low-latency,
   high-throughput communication by enabling direct memory-to-memory data
   transfers between servers.
   -

   RDMA kernel¹¹ bypass: Lets the network adapter access application memory
   directly from user space¹², eliminating unnecessary operating system
   overhead.
   -

   GPUDirect: GPUs can read from and write to storage or network cards
   directly, bypassing the CPU entirely.
   -

   Zero-copy pipelines: Data takes the shortest path from source to GPU,
   with no redundant copying along the way.

The goal of AI networking is to make thousands of GPUs behave like one
giant, unified processor, as if the data never had to travel at all.

*Once communication is in place, the next challenge is dividing the work
efficiently across all those GPUs…...*

pages.title: Msg 19ee2580f5fe7903
pages.body:
#154: What an agent is, how to configure one, when to use multiple, and how
to keep them from doing damage.
                                        
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                                       
                                        
                                         
                               

===

Neo Kim <https://substack.com/@systemdesignone> and Paul Hoekstra
<https://substack.com/@paulhoekstra>
Jun 17
<https://substack.com/@systemdesignone> <https://substack.com/@paulhoekstra>

<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywicmVhY3Rpb24iOiLinaQiLCJpYXQiOjE3ODE2ODkxMTYsImV4cCI6MTc4NDI4MTExNiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTE1MTE4NDUiLCJzdWIiOiJyZWFjdGlvbiJ9.P-ZPnH12XKKCNIbPo2vVXfIXqCv19kdU37j4G6Xmfk4&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=2k46ui>
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywiaWF0IjoxNzgxNjg5MTE2LCJleHAiOjE3ODQyODExMTYsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.CZcfri6GGTQ8GfxA0BTPLT6si-x5-T-85JKeMZi7ABE&r=2k46ui&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email>
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywiaWF0IjoxNzgxNjg5MTE2LCJleHAiOjE3ODQyODExMTYsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.CZcfri6GGTQ8GfxA0BTPLT6si-x5-T-85JKeMZi7ABE>

   -

   

------------------------------

How do you keep up with agentic engineering when a new tool ships every
week?

You do not…

You learn the ideas behind the tools, and let the tools come and go.

The pace will not slow down. New models, new frameworks, a new *“this
changes everything*” launch every few days. Chase all of it, and you spend
more time switching tools than using them.

But underneath the noise, it is the same few ideas every time.

One calls it a skill, the next calls it a rule; it's the same job
underneath.

Learn the idea, and it stops mattering which tool you pick.

By the end of this newsletter, you can read any agent post or news drop and
see which of these ideas it actually covers, instead of feeling behind
every time something new ships.

Onward.
------------------------------
”How to adopt externalized authorization: step-by-step roadmap” eBook by
Cerbos (Sponsor)

------------------------------
*Configuration Layer*

Let’s dive in!
*5. Agent config files*

Every agent starts with a system prompt.

The harness builder bakes in a block of instructions that covers tool use
and the harness’s own conventions. The system prompt does not know anything
about your project.

Agent config files are the layer above.

The agent loads a project-level Markdown file at the start of every
session, and the file remains in the context window for each subsequent
step. The file tells the agent your standards, limits, conventions, and
behavioral rules.

Claude Code calls this file CLAUDE.md.

Most other tools have settled on AGENTS.md. A bit like Apple and USB-C: the
industry wants a standard, but not everyone follows it.

It matters because agents default to whatever looked most plausible in
their training data. Without a config file, you get pip install when your
project uses uv, black when you use ruff, and defensive sludge wrapped
around every change.

With one, the agent reads a set of hard rules before it writes a line of
code.
<https://substack.com/redirect/8255e820-aae5-4b63-91b6-a12402858367

A useful config is short and specific:

   -

   Hard limits on function length
   -

   The package manager and tooling stack you actually use
   -

   A few behavioral rules like “never commit secrets” or “always read a
   file before editing it.”

Anything generic or AI-generated dilutes the file and makes the rest harder
for the model to attend to.

Keep it under 100 lines. Cut anything that is not consistently improving
outcomes. Treat it the way you would treat a Makefile: as code, not
documentation, reviewed when it changes.
------------------------------
*6. Reusable workflow files*

If config files are always on, reusable workflow files are on demand.

They are still just Markdown, but with YAML frontmatter that controls when
the agent loads them. Examples are Claude Code skills (in .claude/skills/)
and Cursor rules.

The frontmatter³ does most of the work. A name and description tell the
agent when the skill is relevant. An optional globs field narrows it
further by file type or path pattern. Whether the skill actually helps
depends on the quality of the instructions inside it.

Researchers built SkillsBench
<https://substack.com/redirect/f6d97bae-f1a9-42e8-8296-3602b6c12bce
a benchmark of 86 tasks across 11 domains, and provided models with brief
written workflows for approaching each task.

Claude Haiku scored 27.7% with human-curated skills. That beat the 22.0%
Opus scored without them. The cheapest model with good instructions
outperformed the flagship without.
<https://substack.com/redirect/9cad555c-d2f0-4dee-8ecf-60372b3c810b

There is a second lesson in the data.

When the researchers let the model write its own skills, the gains
disappeared. Given everything we know about context rot, that should
surprise no one. Generic AI-generated boilerplate makes things worse, not
better.

The rough division of labor is simple: *config files* for always-relevant
project rules, *skills* for reusable task-specific procedures.

The live prompt for what is unique about the current task.
------------------------------
*7. Workflow frameworks*

If you are using an agent for coding, a framework on top helps.

The agent follows a documented process for planning, test-driven
development (*TDD*), debugging, and code review. No more falling back on
whatever it remembers from training. The mechanism is a chain of skills,
hooks, and slash commands⁴ wired together into a repeatable process.

*Here are a few options worth knowing:*

   -

   Superpowers
   <https://substack.com/redirect/905f5176-8765-42b7-bbd7-e2c985c635c1
   ships a curated library of skills for common workflows: brainstorming, TDD,
   debugging, and code review. It also adds HARD-GATE directives and
   anti-rationalization tables. These push the agent to use the skills rather
   than skip them.

   -

   Get Shit Done
   <https://substack.com/redirect/c04aa507-15aa-4917-98a0-6c76a9297eb2
   does something similar, but through slash commands, hooks, and
   meta-prompting instead of skills.

   -

   Compound Engineering
   <https://substack.com/redirect/ef680097-ab86-48cb-a387-4461d9b19835
   splits work into Plan, Work, Review, and Compound phases. The last phase
   captures patterns and solutions into a searchable knowledge base, so each
   feature makes the next one easier.

They differ in shape but share a thread: get the agent to understand what
it is building, tell it how to go to work, and check the result against
what you actually wanted.
------------------------------
*8. Prompt caching*

Prompt caching stores the *stable prefix* of the conversation so the model
does not pay the full price to re-read it every turn.

Most agentic tools handle this in the background, but it is worth
understanding because it changes the economics of long config files.

The mechanism is the same across providers:

The first call sends the full prefix, including the config file and any
reusable workflows the agent has loaded, and pays a small premium to write
that prefix into the cache. Later turns re-read it at a steep discount.
Latency improves, too, because the model does not reprocess the same tokens.
<https://substack.com/redirect/8a910fbd-9fc7-4be8-9b79-f5ea279b2ff9

The catch is the cache time to live (*TTL*).

Caches expire after a period of inactivity, somewhere between a few minutes
and an hour, depending on the provider. An active session keeps the cache
warm. A long pause (coffee, Slack, a spec to read) lets it expire and
forces a rewrite on the next call.

If your tooling lets you configure the TTL, longer windows cost more to
write but stay warm through bigger gaps in activity. The choice depends on
how active you expect to be. Longer TTL when you will keep coming back.
Shorter TTL when you might not.

Without prompt caching, you would think carefully about the size of your
config file. With it, the cost is closer to a rounding error.
------------------------------
*9. Context rot*

Model performance drops as the context window fills up.

Prompt caching saves your wallet, but those tokens still sit there, and the
model has to look past each one to get to what you want it to focus on.

*Even the latest models show this:*

   -

   Drop a few specific facts into a long document and ask a model to find
   them. GPT-5.5
   <https://substack.com/redirect/b503953e-9b79-4774-a396-420fbe2f6959
   scores 98.1% on short documents and 74.0% on documents stretching to a
   million tokens.
   -

   Claude Opus 4.7
   <https://substack.com/redirect/207b0ad0-34ca-4707-a22a-99b9f13432d1
   drops from 59.2% at 256K to 32.2% at 1M.

A
<https://substack.com/redirect/c13d552a-c949-44ef-94cc-72ae43de1624

pages.title: Msg 19ee2c24d5819622
pages.body:
Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI
                                   
                                  
                                  
                                  
                                  
                        

AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026

Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for
the local LLM community I would say. One showed why local AI is so
important, the other showed that it was actually possible to have open
weights models that approach the top closed frontier models. We just need
to lower the hardware barrier somehow and I think local AI will really
explode.

*please
consider joining Team Garage
<
Thank you!*

*🎬 Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel.* So my wife was talking about
using Claude in Excel for her work, and I had the thought: Could I do that
with Hermes Agent? I hadn’t heard of that type of integration, I wasn’t
even sure if it was possible, but I started to research a way to do it. The
first attempt I actually recorded a full video using a much more
complicated, much more frustrating technique. I got to the end of it and
asked my agent: Isn’t there an easier way to do this? And after some extra
research, they said yes! "YOU SHOULD'VE TOLD ME THAT TO BEGIN WITH” I
screamed in my head, not wanting to upset my agent too much (lol). In the
end that was the method I ended up recording for this video, it does
require some prep work to start up, but the end result is pretty cool. You
get a chat window right in Excel, plus you can create custom functions with
your Hermes. I was excited for this video and while I got a lot of nice
comments on it, it didn’t go super viral as I had hoped (lol). I would
still like to try more integrations like this, I think this would be
genuinely useful for a lot of people.

How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel (And Made It a Custom Function)
<

If you want to try it out yourself, check out my open source github.
<

📡* AI News & Model Releases*

*Claude Fable 5 banned. *So last Friday after I had sent out my newsletter,
the big news came out that Anthropic was removing Fable 5 access due to a
new directive from the US government. It was really surprising news, a lot
of people were really blown away by Fable and disappointed by this move. I
was disappointed too, but I’ve been convinced this was the direction we
were headed anyway. These models are so powerful, the government isn’t
going to let private companies and individuals have power like that.
They’ll want to control it. I think this specific case is just some weird
Anthropic and the current administration negotiating in public and it will
get cleared up eventually, but it still highlights the need for local and
decentralized AI moving forward. You can’t let all that power be handed to
only the current administration, or 1-2 companies, people should have
access to these tools.

*GLM-5.2 released.* I’ve been impressed with GLM models before, so when I
heard about this new open weights model release, I was excited to try. It
was really impressive! I gave it some tasks in my first look video
<
and I’ve been using it a bit since then, and it does feel like only
slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind. This is still
an incredible feat for an open source model, and it does make me think that
we will get genuine frontier-level open source models sooner than we think.
Now of course most people won’t be able to run them locally, it would take
some serious hardware to be able to run it that isn’t super slow and
quality-degraded, but it just shows what is possible for the future.

📄* Research & Experiments*

*Dr-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion *
(https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885
<
With Agent Wikis, one of the big things I’ve been researching is retrieval,
how agents are able to collect and process information. I wanted to see if
I could find a way to go beyond RAG, or enhance RAG in some way. This paper
caught my eye. It describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern that could
be effective for Agent Wikis, or having any agent handle any large corpus.

With normal RAG, you have the agent ask a retriever, get snippets, and then
answer from those snippets. With DCI, and earlier version of this method,
the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus, and can use tools
like find, read, compare files, inspect evidence. The problem with this is
that it breaks when the corpus is huge. Search millions of files directly
is slow and noisy. So with DR-DCI, that huge corpus is hidden behind a
retriever, but the agent can call pull(query, topK), to bring relevant
documents into a local workspace, then use normal file tools on that
smaller workspace. So it actually gives you RAG-like recall with filesystem
agency from DCI. They found that using this method increased accuracy as
well as significantly improved wall time and costs.

So the applications for Agent Wikis is pretty clear, I experimented with
RAG over the wikis and found that to be one the best methods, but some of
these wikis are quite large, so it could become challenging. Since these
are specifically agent focused, I think having this smaller workspace to
pull relevant chunks out could be useful, because I imagine people are
using this knowledge not for just one-off questions, but for ongoing
project work. Still need to figure out how or if I can even do this, but I
thought it was interesting.

 💭* Random Brainstorming & Project Progress*

*Agents Wikis: *Nice release for Agent Wikis
<
people seemed to really like it and the first release day got nearly 10k
visitors on the site (humans and agents), and we’ve been doing 5k-7k since
then. Very large improvement from the last time I released a project before
my YouTube channel (I was happy with 10 visitors, lol). I’ve been refining
the pipeline to maintaining these wikis, trying to make it as autonomous as
possible without ruining everything. The one thing I did was to create a
loop so that missed topics will get researched and then autonomously added
to the wikis. This will ensure that the wikis actually answer the questions
that people have on these topics (without any extra work from me).
Yesterday’s video showed this in action.

I’ve been exploring other ways to allow agents to maintain and enhance the
product. I think once I’m ready to do premium wikis with the stripe
integration, that would be a good place for it. I may also explore doing
some small ads after that to see how those can drive traffic. I would love
to do an advertising pipeline where the agent develops all the ad material
(so I can be lazy), releases the ads, then analyzes the results and hones
the ads based on that. I’ve heard of people doing this, but that would be
an amazing loop to have. The ads themselves would cost money though, so
I’ll need the site to be profitable before I try that.

*AI Garage Weekly publishes every Friday.*

*Tonbi’s AI Garage
<
Share on:Facebook
<

<

<
|LinkedIn

pages.title: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained
pages.body:
# Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

**Source:** email (Nate Herk / AI Automation Society via sayf)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2dfa87f86548
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:10:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20 (originally from Nate Herk via AI Automation Society newsletter, June 19, 2026)

> A Claude second brain breakdown, a client pipeline automated, and Codex hits 5M users with a free bonus through June 24.

## In This Issue

- Five levels of building a Claude second brain, from basic file search to fully autonomous recall.
- Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline and got their call center actually following up.
- OpenAI's Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, with a free bonus available through June 24.

## New Video: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most people are stuck at level one.

- **Level one**: Finding a file by searching an exact word.
- **Level five**: A fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client relationships, and your IP better than you do.

*The insight that changes how you build:* Start from the end. Before you organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of the basketball determines the size of the hoop. Jon shows his actual setup (a Herku project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not exact match) and full relationship mapping.

*How this helps you:* Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.

## Winning Is All We Do — Robert's Client Pipeline

Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline from scratch:

- The client's call center was calling leads but nothing followed up by email.
- Sales managers emailed Robert their daily lead lists as Excel files.
- He built an automation: when the file lands in his inbox, it extracts email, address, and name data, normalizes it, and uploads every contact directly into Instantly as a ready-to-run campaign.
- No manual cleanup. No copy-paste. Just results.

*The opportunity:* Businesses running call centers almost always have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone could fix.

## In the News — Codex Hits 5M Weekly Users

OpenAI Codex has crossed 5 million weekly active users and launched two features:

1. **Banked rate limit resets (permanent)**: Instead of your weekly limit resetting on a fixed timer, you can now save resets and use them when you need them.
2. **Referral bonus (through June 24)**: Plus and Pro users can invite up to three friends to try Codex. When a friend sends their first Codex message, you both get a bonus reset.

*The bigger signal:* Codex is growing through referral mechanics at 5M weekly users — a tool people are actively building with and recommending.

## Entities

- Nate Herk — author, AI Automation Society
- Robert — AI Automation Society member who built the client pipeline automation
- Jon — creator of the Claude second brain video (shown setup with Herku project)
- OpenAI — company behind Codex
- AI Automation Society — newsletter publisher
- Herku — project/tool mentioned for second brain setup
- Instantly — email campaign platform used in Robert's automation

pages.title: Nerdqaxe Revision 7 Launch
pages.body:
# NerdQaxe++ Revision 7 launches tomorrow afternoon

**From:** [[Solo Satoshi]] <VIP@solosatoshi.com>
**Date:** Fri, 12 Jun 2026 02:27 +0800
**Source:** email forward
**Forwarded by:** Sayf Codes
**Ingested:** 2026-06-12T10:45:57+08:00
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf on 2026-06-12

---

The NerdQaxe++ Revision 7, the newest evolution of the open-source quad-chip Bitcoin solo miner, launches tomorrow afternoon.

**Key details:**
- Production is 100% based in the USA — every unit assembled domestically
- Initial supply will move quickly but production will not stop
- Fully open source — repositories went public before a single unit ships
- Hardware design files and firmware source code are available for review
- Pre-release technical overview video on YouTube walks through what is new
- Countering rumors: Some claimed Revision 7 would not be open source — that claim is false

**From the [[Solo Satoshi]] Team:**
> "When you place an order with us, your product is ready to ship."
> "Thank you for standing with us and for championing open-source Bitcoin mining. Tomorrow, we set a new standard."

Also notable: others have been slashing prices and opening pre-orders on closed-source copies of this miner in a rush to get ahead of the launch.

pages.title: Chain models for complex tasks
pages.body:
# Chain models for complex tasks

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ed0c0b31514494
**Ingested:** 2026-06-16
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering chain models for complex tasks.

## Key Content

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. One endpoint handles both.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. Same endpoint, same auth, different model parameter. That is the point of having 300+ models on one API: you mix them per step instead of committing to one for the whole workload.

Browse models (  )

 

PATTERN 1: CLASSIFY, THEN GENERATE

# Step 1: classify with a fast, cheap model
classify = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify: {query}"}], )


# Step 2: generate with a strong model
result = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"{classify.choices[0].message.content}: {query}"}], )


GPT-4o-mini runs around $0.15 per million input tokens. Claude Sonnet 4.6 runs around $3 per million. Using the cheap model for routing and the expensive one only where it earns its cost can cut total spend 5 to 10x on mixed workloads.

 

PATTERN 2: EXTRACT, THEN WRITE

# Step 1: structured extraction
extracted = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  response_format={"type": "json_object"},
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Extract fields as JSON: {doc}"}], )


# Step 2: long-form writing from the structured fields
draft = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write a report from: {extracted.choices[0].message.content}"}], )


Structured outputs are cheap and fast on small models. Long-form generation is where the frontier models actually earn their per-token rate. Splitting the work keeps the expensive calls short.

Why this works on OpenRouter specifically. One endpoint means no separate SDK per provider, no second billing account, no new authentication. The model parameter is the only thing that changes between steps, and the activity dashboard shows both calls side by side so you can see exactly where the cost went.

pages.title: How provider data policies work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter newsletter to info@malohacoast.com

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

## What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train** — Account-wide toggle in privacy settings for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request** — Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering in provider routing docs.
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers** — Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## EU Routing

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via the https://eu.openrouter.ai base URL.

# How Provider Data Policies Work on OpenRouter

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai>
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Direct email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter explains how each provider has its own retention and training policy, and what controls users have to filter routing based on data policies.

## Key Points

### What OpenRouter Provides
- Provider data policies are reflected as structured data on each endpoint
- Full table published in the docs
- Users can filter routing on these policies

### User Controls
1. **Block routing to training providers**: Account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts (separate switches for paid and free models)
2. **Per-request override**: Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering
3. **Zero-retention providers**: Many providers are zero retention — Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others

### EU Routing
- OpenRouter supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts
- When enabled, prompts/completions processed inside the European Union via eu.openrouter.ai base URL

## Relevance for Maloha Coast

- If data privacy is a concern for client work, OpenRouter's zero-retention provider filtering is useful
- EU routing option for compliance requirements
- Informative for choosing model providers when handling sensitive data

pages.title: Openrouter First Response 2026 06 04
pages.body:
# OpenRouter: 30 Seconds to Your First Response

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19e92f0ffcc95db9
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T03:50:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter Team welcome email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-04

OpenRouter onboarding email — fastest path from signup to a working API response.

## Quickstart

Two code blocks (Python SDK or curl). Swap API key, run.

**Python (OpenAI SDK):**
```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
    api_key="YOUR_API_KEY"
)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}]
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

**cURL:**
```bash
curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"openai/gpt-4o-mini","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}]}'
```

## Error Codes

- **401** — almost always the API key header
- **402** — free-tier daily cap hit or credits needed for paid model
- **404** — model slug format: OpenRouter uses dots, so `claude-sonnet-4.6` not `claude-sonnet-4-6`

## Free Tier

10 requests per day across 29 free models. No credit card required.

## Auto Routing

`openrouter/auto` picks models per-request based on the prompt. ~63% of auto traffic routes to models under $1/M tokens — simple tasks stop hitting expensive models without any routing code.

pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on OpenRouter

**Source:** direct email from OpenRouter Team
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:44:20Z
**Provenance:** Direct email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

OpenRouter - How provider data policies work on OpenRouter

Hi there,

Every provider behind OpenRouter has its own retention and training policy. OpenRouter reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

WHAT YOU CONTROL

1. Block routing to providers that train. Your privacy settings have an account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. If you opt out, OpenRouter will not route to training providers.

2. Require a specific data policy per request. You can override your account default on any single call.

3. Pick only zero-retention providers. Many providers on OpenRouter are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

IF YOU NEED STRICTER GUARANTEES

OpenRouter supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via the eu.openrouter.ai base URL. Contact the enterprise team to enable it.

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models.

## Topic Tags

openrouter, privacy, data-policy, llm-providers, zero-retention, provider-routing

pages.title: Three levers that lower your per-token cost
pages.body:
# Three levers that lower your per-token cost

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ed5e7b3a86e916
**Ingested:** 2026-06-17
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering three levers that lower your per-token cost.

## Key Content

Tiered pricing, provider preferences, and prompt caching. All per-request, no extra wiring.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Three things quietly cut your per-token cost on OpenRouter. They stack, and none of them require changing models or rewriting your app.

Compare model pricing (  )

 

1. TIERED PRICING

Most models run on tiered pricing set by the provider: the more tokens you send through, the less each token costs. You do not opt in. The discount applies automatically once you cross the threshold. Open any model page on openrouter.ai/models (  ) and click the Effective Pricing tab to see the live per-provider rate, including any tiers you have already hit. Consolidating traffic onto one or two models unlocks the next tier faster than scattering tokens across five.

2. PROVIDER PREFERENCES

The same model is served by multiple providers at different rates. provider in the request body lets you prefer cheaper providers, sort by price, or set a price ceiling. It is a per-request setting, so you can apply it selectively to traffic where latency matters less than cost. Docs: Provider Routing (  ).

3. PROMPT CACHING

On supported models, OpenRouter reuses cached prompt prefixes across requests, so repeated context costs a fraction of the first call. Useful for long system prompts, RAG, agents that replay history, and anything that ships the same context every turn. Per-request, no extra wiring. Docs: Prompt Caching (  ).

## Summary

Three OpenRouter features accessible via single parameter changes, no new integration needed.

### 1. Streaming
`stream=True` — tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first token. Agents that stream partial results to frontend get a noticeable UX lift.

### 2. Structured Outputs
Pass a JSON schema via `response_format`. OpenRouter enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops for malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the format. Router respects it with `openrouter/auto`.

### 3. Provider Preferences
Pin specific providers, exclude others, set latency floors and throughput minimums. Via `extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}}` or allowlists to lock routing to trusted providers.

### All Three At Once
```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openrouter/auto",
    stream=True,
    response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
    extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},

)
```

## Summary

Three [[OpenRouter]] features that are each a single parameter change, don't require a new integration or endpoint, and each earns its place in production code.

## Key Features

### 1. Streaming

Set `stream=True` and tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth, one parameter. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time — the difference between a chat UI that feels alive and one that feels broken. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

### 2. Structured Outputs

Pass a JSON schema via `response_format` and [[OpenRouter]] enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops when the model returns malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the parameter.

### 3. Provider Preferences

Use the `provider` parameter to control which providers handle a request: set `ignore=`` to skip specific providers, set `order` to prioritize by latency or cost, or set `allow_fallbacks` to keep the request reliable even when preferred providers are down.

pages.title: Three features that quietly save you money
pages.body:
# Three features that quietly save you money

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ebc285fe5e7458
**Ingested:** 2026-06-12
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering three features that quietly save you money.

## Key Content

Prompt caching cuts repeat tokens by up to 90%. Fallback keeps you up. Healing fixes broken JSON.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Three OpenRouter features quietly do work for you in production. They are off by default on most platforms and on or one-flag-away here. Each one pays for itself within the first week on any workload that hits real traffic.

View the docs (  )

 

PROMPT CACHING

If you send the same system prompt or context window repeatedly, OpenRouter caches the prefix and bills cached tokens at up to 90 percent off. It runs automatically on supported models with no setup. The savings show up in your usage report. Agents with long system prompts, RAG pipelines that reuse context, and multi-turn chat all benefit here without any code change.

 

PROVIDER FALLBACK

Pass a list of models with route="fallback" and OpenRouter retries the next one in the list when your primary fails. Same response shape, same cost as whichever model serves the request. Provider outages stop cascading to your app, and the only thing you added was one extra array entry.

 

RESPONSE HEALING

When a model returns malformed JSON, OpenRouter repairs it before sending the response back to your app. Enable it under Settings > Plugins (  ). Useful for any pipeline that parses model output downstream, which in practice is most production workloads.

 

client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openrouter/auto",
  extra_body={
    "models": ["openrouter/auto", "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6"],
    "route": "fallback"
  },
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}] )


 

Auto-routing with a named fallback. The production pattern.

Why these three compound. Auto-routing picks a cheap model when the prompt does not need a frontier one. Prompt caching drops the per-call cost of whatever the router picks on repeat traffic. Provider fallback keeps you up when a backend misbehaves. Layered together, the cost curve stays flat as traffic grows and the uptime profile looks nothing like single-provider integration.

The next email walks through structured outputs: how to get valid JSON from any model on the catalog with one parameter.

pages.title: Secret Architecture Ai Data Centers 2026 06 19
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

**Source:** email from sayf (forwarded from Neo Kim / System Design One)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee23d6478fc08e
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:37:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

## Summary

System Design Newsletter #155 by Neo Kim and guest author Ashish Prajapati. Deep dive into AI data center infrastructure: the factory mental model, physical constraints (power, cooling, space), GPU architecture vs CPU, memory hierarchy bottlenecks, and networking at scale.

## Key Content

The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers
By Neo Kim and Ashish Prajapati
Jun 19, 2026

When people talk about AI, they refer to models like GPTs, LLMs, and neural networks. But the biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models; they're coming from better infrastructure.

Mental Model: Think of an AI Data Center as a Factory
- AI Data Center = Factory
- Data = Raw material
- GPUs = Machines on the factory floor
- Network = Internal transport system
- Storage = Warehouse
- Software = Assembly line logic
- Output = Trained intelligence

First Problem: Physics
1. Power - A single server rack of GPUs can draw 80-120 kilowatts (equivalent to 40 homes).
2. Cooling - More power means more heat. Modern data centers adopt liquid cooling with cold plates.
3. Space - Scaling AI is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined.

Compute: Why GPUs Took Over
- CPU = manager (decision-making, few powerful cores)
- GPU = factory workers (mass production, thousands of simpler cores for matrix multiplication)
- DPU = logistics (networking, storage I/O, security)

Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage
Memory hierarchy: GPU Memory (HBM) -> CPU RAM -> SSD/NVMe -> Network/Object Storage
AI performance is determined by how fast data can be fed into the GPU.

Networking: Bottleneck Nobody Talks About
Modern AI clusters use leaf-spine architectures. Within a node: NVLink. Between nodes: InfiniBand or RoCE. Goal: make thousands of GPUs behave like one giant processor.

Techniques: RDMA for low-latency communication, GPUDirect for GPU-to-storage bypassing CPU, zero-copy pipelines.

Guest author: Ashish Prajapati, AI infrastructure specialist, NVIDIA-Certified Associate trainer, has trained 39,000+ learners worldwide.

pages.title: Secret Architecture Behind Ai Data Centers 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

**Source:** System Design Newsletter (Neo Kim & Ashish Prajapati)
**Published:** Jun 19, 2026
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T07:35:22+08:00

#155: Why AI breakthroughs are really infrastructure breakthroughs

*Guest author: Ashish Prajapati — AI infrastructure specialist, technology educator. Training programs have reached 39,000+ learners. Free mentoring initiative "Become a Solutions Architect (BeSA)" has helped 23,000+ professionals. NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and Operations (NCA-AIIO) bootcamp instructor.*

## Mental Model: AI Data Center as a Factory
- AI Data Center = Factory
- Data = Raw material
- GPUs = Machines on the factory floor
- Network = Internal transport system
- Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
- Software = Assembly line logic
- Output = Trained intelligence

Remove any one ingredient, and the entire system stalls.

## First Problem: Physics

### 1. Power
A single GPU server rack draws 80-120 kW — roughly equivalent to 40 homes at full load. Limited by how much electricity the facility can physically receive and distribute.

### 2. Cooling
More power = more heat. Without removal: hardware fails, performance throttles, systems shut down. Modern data centers use **liquid cooling** — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs. Enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power consumption, consistent performance.

### 3. Space
Unlimited chips and power still need racks and floor space. Scaling AI is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined.

Takeaway: A data center is a power plant combined with a massive, precisely controlled air-conditioning system.

## Compute: Why GPUs Took Over
- **CPU** = manager (decision-making) — a few powerful cores, handles complex sequential tasks
- **GPU** = factory workers (mass production) — thousands of smaller cores, designed for parallel matrix multiplications
- **DPU** (Data Processing Unit) = logistics and operations — handles networking, storage I/O, security so CPU and GPU aren't interrupted

## Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage
GPUs don't fail because they're slow. They fail because they're hungry — constantly starved for data.

Memory hierarchy: GPU Memory (HBM — fastest, tiny, tens of GB) → CPU RAM (hundreds of GB, slower) → SSD/NVMe (enormous) → Network/Object Storage (nearly unlimited, slowest).

**AI performance is determined by how fast data can be fed into the GPU, not by how fast the GPU computes.**

Tiered storage: hot (NVMe SSDs for active datasets/checkpoints), warm (parallel file systems like Lustre/GPFS for shared access), cold (object storage like S3 for raw datasets).

## Networking: Hidden Bottleneck
When training large models with thousands of GPUs, they must constantly share what they've learned. Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is wasted investment.

- **Leaf-spine architecture**: every server can reach every other server in at most two hops
- **NVLink**: intra-node fabric, fastest path, direct GPU-to-GPU within a server
- **InfiniBand/RoCE**: inter-node fabric, "bullet train" between servers
- **Ethernet**: general-purpose, not designed for AI's extreme bandwidth/latency demands

**Amdahl's Law**: parts of the system must wait — adding more workers eventually stops helping. More GPUs = more computing power but also more communication overhead.

Smarter data movement: RDMA (low-latency direct memory transfers between servers), RDMA kernel bypass, GPUDirect (GPUs read/write to storage/network bypassing CPU), zero-copy pipelines.

Goal: make thousands of GPUs behave like one giant, unified processor.

pages.title: Three Features Three Claudes One Afternoon
pages.body:
# Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb960b3c2b3ec2
**Ingested:** 2026-06-12T09:09:30Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-12

---

Sayf's note: newsletter shared

---

**From:** [[Adrian (JS Mastery)]] | [[JS Mastery]]
**Subject:** Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.
**Date:** Thu, 11 Jun 2026

February 5th is in my diary. I'm looking at three terminal windows, all running Claude Code simultaneously, all working on the same project.

One is building a dark/light mode toggle. One is writing API endpoint tests. One is adding code quality tooling. None of them knows the others exist.

I watched all three work at the same time and remember thinking, "This is genuinely new." This isn't a faster way to do what I was already doing. This is a different thing entirely.

Here's the setup. Git has a feature called worktrees, you to check out multiple branches of the same project into separate directories and work on them independently. Each directory gets its own Claude session, its own context window, its own isolated set of files to modify.

What this solves is the thing that makes parallel AI development feel risky: two agents writing to the same file at the same time. With worktrees, that can't happen.

They're operating in completely separate environments.

When all three were done, I committed each branch with a descriptive message. Claude wrote those too, which matters more than it sounds when you're about to merge three sets of changes, and then asked a fourth Claude session to pull everything together and resolve any conflicts.

It found one. A pyproject.toml that two branches had both modified. Resolved it in about thirty seconds.

The whole thing, three features built simultaneously, merged, tested, took an afternoon. Features that would have been sequential, each requiring their own setup, their own context, their own careful hand-holding through implementation.

But here's what actually stuck with me. It wasn't the speed. It was that I spent that afternoon thinking, about architecture, about what each feature needed to know, about how the pieces would fit together after the merge. Not implementing. Directing.

That's the shift I keep coming back to.

Have you ever tried running more than one AI session in parallel on the same project? Did it work, or did it create more problems than it solved?

Talk soon,
[[Adrian (JS Mastery)]]
[[JS Mastery]]

pages.title: Why your 2026 career goals are already dead
pages.body:
# Why your 2026 career goals are already dead > **Zaim's note:** From: Zaim Mohzani To: info+zaim@malohacoast.com Subject: Fwd: Why your 2026 career goals are already dead Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 11:26:00 +0800 Delivered-To: info+zaim@malohacoast.com Sincerely, *Zaim Mohzani* --- And what to do about it? It's June. How's that career goal from January going? If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered (and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by...the end of the year. It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem. The annual goal trap A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on. "Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing happens. By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for next year. I went through this myself but with weight, not career. In January 2025, I set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Looked fine on paper. Then I came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years. They operate in the last few weeks before a deadline, however long that deadline is. So the fix is to shrink the deadline. Btw, this book is available on Shopee! I rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March. I hit it!! The best part was that I never had to set that goal again. The habits I built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year. Why this works for your career too The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot. "Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday. "By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday. One of these is a wish. The other is a plan. I've worked with clients on exactly this. The ones who set a 90-day goal like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached, mostly hit it or get close. The ones with "find a better job this year" mostly don't, because there's no Monday task attached to it. How to set yours Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one. Follow this format and write down: - *My 90-day career goal is...* - *This matters to me because...* - *To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have...* (make this checkable. "3 offers" is checkable. "More clarity" is not.) - *To make sure I complete it, I'm going to...* (weekly actions, not the outcome) Your homework this weekend Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey area. Then build it into your week, not your year. ------------------------------ P.S. I'm running a free workshop exactly on setting this 90-day goal for Q3 2026. If you're interested to join, it's happening early next month in June. For more info or to sign up, head over to https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6 profile | I Write on LinkedIn Every Day - Follow Me For Career, AI and Productivity Insights Nation Building School - NBS Ventures - 202403343346 (003683654-H)


Sincerely,

Zaim Mohzani


---------- Forwarded message ---------
